./paper/paper2/skeleton_v1/SKELETON.md:81:- Noise: C2C (per-forward Gaussian) + D2D (fixed per instance)
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v1/SKELETON.md:83:- Nonlinearity: NL_LTP/NL_LTD from Work 1
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v1/SKELETON.md:179:| D2D noise σ | σ_d2d | [TBD] | Work 1 |
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/02_related.md:7:A persistent limitation across these frameworks is the treatment of device noise as statistically homogeneous. Most simulators inject i.i.d. Gaussian perturbations to conductances or weights, which captures C2C variability but obscures the spatially structured nature of D2D mismatch maps. In fabricated arrays, mismatch is correlated across the wafer, with smooth spatial gradients and die-level offsets that create coherent distortion patterns. Ensemble HAT addresses this by resampling full spatial mismatch maps rather than independent per-cell noise, but the broader simulator landscape has been slow to adopt structured mismatch modeling. This paper assumes spatially structured D2D fields and treats instance overfitting as a first-class failure mode.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/02_related.md:11:Hardware-aware training (HAT) for analog CIM follows the quantization-aware training paradigm: device noise is injected into the forward pass during training so that the optimizer learns weights that are robust to the expected deployment perturbation. Standard HAT fixes a single D2D mismatch realization for the entire training run. This approach is computationally cheap but invites hardware-instance overfitting: the model learns to compensate for one specific spatial distortion and collapses when tested on a fresh array.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/02_related.md:13:Variation-aware training extends standard HAT by sampling multiple mismatch realizations within a batch or epoch, a strategy analogous to domain randomization in sim-to-real robotics. Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) and related flat-minimum seekers have been proposed as generic robustness enhancers, though their parameter-space isotropy assumptions do not map cleanly onto the structured mismatch manifolds of analog arrays. Ensemble HAT, which resamples the full D2D map at every epoch, recovers strong fresh-instance accuracy under moderate nonlinearity ($\text{NL}=1.0$), establishing that instance overfitting is solvable when the surrogate remains locally valid. What remains unknown—and what this paper addresses—is whether any HAT variant survives once the nonlinearity invalidates the local surrogate approximation.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/03_methods.md:5:All experiments are executed inside the compute-ViT framework, a PyTorch-based behavioral simulator that maps vision-transformer weights to differential conductance pairs and injects device non-idealities during training. We use Tiny-ViT V4 (approximately $5\,\text{M}$ parameters) as the backbone, trained from an ImageNet-pretrained initialization on CIFAR-10 for $100$ epochs with AdamW ($\text{lr}=5\times 10^{-4}$, weight decay $0.05$), cosine annealing, and batch size $64$. The analog-mapping rules convert each linear and convolutional layer to differential conductance arrays with six-bit ADC readout, baseline D2D mismatch variance $\sigma^{2}_{\text{D2D}}=(5\%)^{2}$, and C2C variability drawn from a Gaussian distribution. These settings mirror the canonical protocol established in prior work.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/03_methods.md:27:The analog array stores conductances, not weights. The recovered weight is $\hat{W}_{ij}=s_{\ell}\,G_{\text{eff},ij}$, where $s_{\ell}=w_{\max}/(G_{\max}-G_{\min})$. Under D2D mismatch field $M$ and C2C noise $\varepsilon$,
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/03_methods.md:38:> Under the first-order $\text{NL}=2.0$ surrogate, the QKV and output-projection gradients acquire a state-dependent asymmetry that couples the learned spatial filters to the particular D2D pattern experienced during training. When the D2D pattern changes at deployment, the attention geometry—which is the core representational engine of the transformer—ceases to function. The MLP path, even when perfectly linearized, cannot compensate for a broken attention mechanism. Consequently, no training-objective modification within the first-order surrogate family can raise fresh-instance accuracy above a structural ceiling near $30\%$.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/03_methods.md:50:Because $|G_{Q}|$ is spatially structured by the D2D field $M$, the effective learning rate for each element of $W_{Q}$ becomes position-dependent. Over many epochs, the optimizer adapts $W_{Q}$ to this modulation, effectively pre-distorting the query matrix so that the product $XW_{Q}$ compensates for the known spatial signature of $M$. The critical issue is that the query-key inner product $QK^{\top}$ is bilinear in the weights. If $W_{Q}$ and $W_{K}$ are each adapted to a different spatial distortion (because their conductance arrays have independent D2D realizations), the compensation does not transfer. The softmax operator is highly sensitive to the relative magnitudes of the inner products; a small shift in $W_{Q}$ or $W_{K}$ can re-rank the attention scores and destroy token-to-token correspondence.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/03_methods.md:58:because the state-dependent gradient scaling biases the matrices toward the dominant singular vectors associated with large-conductance regions of the D2D map. This rank collapse reduces the expressive capacity of the attention mechanism and makes the geometry fragile to D2D perturbation.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/03_methods.md:78:Under $\text{NL}=2.0$, the mean effective conductance is $\mathbb{E}_{G}[\hat{G}]=\mathbb{E}_{G}[G^{2}]=\text{Var}(G)+\mathbb{E}[G]^{2}$, which depends on the second moment of the conductance distribution. The D2D field $M$ changes both the mean and the variance of $G_{\text{eff}}$ across the array. The learned scale factor $s_{\ell}$ therefore calibrates to the training-time second moment under $M_{0}$. At deployment, the second moment under $M'\neq M_{0}$ differs, and the scale recovery introduces a systematic gain mismatch that is invisible to source-domain accuracy (because the model trained on $M_{0}$ has already adapted to the miscalibrated $s_{\ell}$) but fatal to fresh-instance transfer.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/03_methods.md:96:1. **Standard HAT (fixed mask, $\text{NL}=2.0$ everywhere).** Baseline with a single D2D realization held constant throughout training.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/03_methods.md:99:4. **Joint MLP-linear + Ensemble HAT.** Lane 2 combined with per-epoch D2D resampling and warm-start from the canonical $\text{NL}=1.0$ checkpoint.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/03_methods.md:139:Ensemble HAT resamples the full spatial D2D mismatch map $M\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^{2}_{\text{D2D}})$ at the beginning of every training epoch. This exposes the optimizer to the deployment distribution rather than a single instance, preventing hardware-instance overfitting. The protocol uses block-stationary resampling (one map per layer type) and AdamW with cosine annealing. Under moderate nonlinearity ($\text{NL}=1.0$), ensemble HAT recovers strong fresh-instance accuracy, validating that the protocol is operational when the surrogate remains locally valid.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/03_methods.md:143:The primary generalization metric is fresh-instance accuracy: after training concludes, the checkpoint is evaluated on $n=10$ independent unseen D2D realizations ("arrays"), with $5$ Monte Carlo forward passes per instance. The primary metric is the mean accuracy across the $10$ per-instance means; uncertainty is reported as the standard deviation across the $10$ per-instance means. Secondary metrics include source-domain (in-distribution) accuracy and per-instance variance. This protocol distinguishes same-instance overfitting from genuine distributional generalization.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/04_experiments.md:9:**Method.** Using the group-wise selector, the `.attn.qkv` layers are designated as the protected group and receive the linear surrogate ($\text{NL}_{\text{LTP}}=1.0$, $\text{NL}_{\text{LTD}}=-1.0$). All other analog layers (`.attn.proj`, `.mlp.fc1`, `.mlp.fc2`, `patch_embed.*`) retain the severe-NL surrogate ($\text{NL}=2.0$). Training follows the ensemble HAT recipe: per-epoch D2D resampling, $100$ epochs, AdamW with cosine annealing, cold start from the ImageNet-pretrained Tiny-ViT-5M checkpoint. Evaluation uses the canonical fresh-instance protocol ($10$ instances × $5$ MC passes).
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/04_experiments.md:11:**Pre-registered prediction.** Under the attention-pathway-dominance hypothesis, QKV-only linearization is predicted to stay near the $\sim 30\%$ ceiling. Linearizing QKV in isolation creates a decoupled optimization: QKV receives clean gradients while the attention output projection receives distorted gradients. The optimizer adapts $W_{V}$ and $W_{O}$ to a distortion that $W_{Q}$ and $W_{K}$ do not sense, tearing the attention mechanism between incompatible objectives. Adding ensemble HAT resampling does not fix this decoupling because the problem is gradient inconsistency, not D2D memorization.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/01_intro.md:5:The gap between device-level promise and system-level performance is dominated by non-ideality. Analog memory cells suffer from cell-to-cell (C2C) variability, device-to-device (D2D) mismatch, retention drift, and—most critically for this study—nonlinear write dynamics. In a nonlinear write regime, the programmed conductance deviates from the target weight in a state-dependent fashion, distorting the effective linear transform that the crossbar is supposed to implement. Unlike additive thermal noise, write nonlinearity introduces multiplicative, input-dependent perturbations that standard training pipelines do not naturally absorb. Hardware-aware training (HAT) mitigates some of these effects by injecting device noise into the forward training path, yet the standard practice of fixing a single D2D mismatch map throughout training invites a subtler failure: the model memorizes one hardware instance rather than learning to generalize across the deployment distribution. When the trained checkpoint encounters a fresh fabricated array with a different fixed mismatch realization, accuracy can collapse to chance level. This hardware-instance overfitting is especially pernicious because it is invisible during conventional same-instance validation, where the model is tested on the same noisy realization it trained against.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/01_intro.md:7:Recent work has made meaningful progress on the mild-nonlinearity regime. The compute-ViT framework introduced a profile-driven behavioral simulation pipeline that maps literature-derived organic device parameters to task-level vision accuracy for ResNet, ConvNeXt, and Tiny-ViT backbones. Within that framework, ensemble hardware-aware training—resampling the D2D mismatch mask at every training epoch rather than reusing a fixed map—recovers strong fresh-instance accuracy on CIFAR-10 with Tiny-ViT under moderate write nonlinearity ($\text{NL}=1.0$). A literature-anchored zero-shot transfer to a calibrated organic device profile confirms the framework's predictive validity. These results establish that analog-mapped vision transformers can in principle achieve deployment-grade accuracy, provided the device stress remains within a certain envelope. What all of these positive results share is a shared assumption: the write nonlinearity is moderate enough that first-order gradient-scaling approximations remain valid during training. The success of ensemble HAT under $\text{NL}=1.0$ is genuine and important, but it does not answer whether the same recipe—or any known variant—survives once the nonlinearity intensifies.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/01_intro.md:11:This paper closes that gap by adopting an explicit falsification posture. We treat the severe-NL ceiling as a testable hypothesis and design three independent candidate mitigations that, if successful, would refute the claim that the barrier is structural. First, we linearize only the MLP blocks, which prior layer-sensitivity analysis identifies as the dominant noise bottleneck, while leaving the attention pathway exposed to full $\text{NL}=2.0$. Second, we linearize the entire model—both MLP and attention layers—to establish an architectural upper bound. Third, we combine MLP-linear protection with ensemble HAT and warm-start from the canonical $\text{NL}=1.0$ checkpoint, creating a joint training recipe that simultaneously addresses severe nonlinearity and instance overfitting. Each mitigation is evaluated under a standardized fresh-instance protocol: ten unseen D2D realizations, five Monte Carlo evaluations per realization, with primary metrics reported as mean ± standard deviation across instances. The compute-ViT framework supplies the common simulation substrate, ensuring that differences across arms reflect genuine capability boundaries rather than implementation variance. By testing three conceptually distinct escape routes, we maximize the chance of refutation and therefore maximize the epistemic value of a negative result.
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/01_intro.md:15:To explain this convergence, we formulate the **Structural-Limit Hypothesis**: under first-order $\text{NL}=2.0$ surrogates, the QKV and output-projection gradients acquire a state-dependent asymmetry that couples the learned spatial filters to the particular D2D pattern experienced during training. When the D2D pattern changes at deployment, the attention geometry—which is the core representational engine of the transformer—ceases to function. The MLP path, even when perfectly linearized, cannot compensate for a broken attention mechanism. The hypothesis rests on three theoretical pillars: (i) gradient-asymmetry-induced rank collapse in Q/K projections; (ii) exponential amplification of surrogate bias by the softmax attention operator; and (iii) instance-specific scale-recovery mismatch that is invisible to source-domain accuracy but fatal to fresh-instance transfer. We derive falsifiable mathematical conditions for each pillar, ensuring that the hypothesis can be revised or refuted as new data arrive.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/SKELETON.md:34:  1. **The analog-CIM promise and the deployment gap.** Analog CIM with organic RRAM offers O(1) MAC energy scaling, but device non-idealities (write nonlinearity, D2D mismatch) create a simulation-to-silicon accuracy cliff.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/SKELETON.md:35:  2. **The two failure modes.** (a) Severe write nonlinearity (NL ≥ 2.0) degrades in-domain accuracy. (b) Hardware-instance overfitting collapses fresh-instance transfer to chance (~10 %) when D2D mismatch is fixed during training.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/SKELETON.md:36:  3. **Ensemble HAT as partial solution.** Epoch-level D2D resampling recovers fresh-instance accuracy to `86.37 ± 1.54 %` under moderate nonlinearity (NL = 1.0), but leaves the severe-NL regime (`NL = 2.0`) unresolved.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/SKELETON.md:57:  2. **Hardware-aware training (HAT) for CIM.** Standard HAT (fixed D2D mask), variation-aware training (Zhu et al., DATE 2020; Liu et al., DAC 2015), and quantization-aware training analogues. Gap: none address severe nonlinearity *and* instance overfitting jointly.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/SKELETON.md:79:  4. **Ensemble HAT protocol.** Per-epoch resampling of full spatial D2D mismatch map `M ~ N(0, σ²_D2D)`; block-stationary schedule; AdamW with warm restart. Contrast with fixed-mask standard HAT (single `M` for all epochs).
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/SKELETON.md:80:  5. **Fresh-instance evaluation protocol.** `10` unseen D2D realizations ("arrays") × `5` Monte Carlo forward passes per instance. Primary metric: mean accuracy across the 10 per-instance means; uncertainty: standard deviation across the 10 per-instance means. Secondary: source-domain (in-distribution) accuracy.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/SKELETON.md:86:  - `σ²_D2D = (5 %)²` (canonical mismatch variance)
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/SKELETON.md:172:  5. **Supplementary Note 5: Correlated-D2D robustness check.** Brief report of i.i.d. vs. ρ = 0.3 / 0.5 AR(1) spatial correlation under Ensemble HAT (NL = 1.0), showing bounded degradation.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/SKELETON.md:180:  - `ρ = 0.3` / `0.5` (correlated D2D check)
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/01_introduction.md:5:The gap between device-level promise and system-level performance is dominated by non-ideality. Analog memory cells suffer from cell-to-cell (C2C) variability, device-to-device (D2D) mismatch, retention drift, and—most critically for this study—nonlinear write dynamics. In a nonlinear write regime, the programmed conductance deviates from the target weight in a state-dependent fashion, distorting the effective linear transform that the crossbar is supposed to implement. Unlike additive thermal noise, write nonlinearity introduces multiplicative, input-dependent perturbations that standard training pipelines do not naturally absorb. Hardware-aware training (HAT) mitigates some of these effects by injecting device noise into the forward training path, yet the standard practice of fixing a single D2D mismatch map throughout training invites a subtler failure: the model memorizes one hardware instance rather than learning to generalize across the deployment distribution. When the trained checkpoint encounters a fresh fabricated array with a different fixed mismatch realization, accuracy can collapse to chance level. This hardware-instance overfitting is especially pernicious because it is invisible during conventional same-instance validation, where the model is tested on the same noisy realization it trained against.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/01_introduction.md:7:Recent work has made meaningful progress on the mild-nonlinearity regime. The compute-ViT framework introduced a profile-driven behavioral simulation pipeline that maps literature-derived organic device parameters to task-level vision accuracy for ResNet, ConvNeXt, and Tiny-ViT backbones. Within that framework, ensemble hardware-aware training—resampling the D2D mismatch mask at every training epoch rather than reusing a fixed map—recovers 86.37 ± 1.54 % fresh-instance accuracy on CIFAR-10 with Tiny-ViT under moderate write nonlinearity (NL = 1.0). A literature-anchored zero-shot transfer to a calibrated OPECT device profile reaches 88.53 ± 0.08 %. These results establish that analog-mapped vision transformers can in principle achieve deployment-grade accuracy, provided the device stress remains within a certain envelope. Iso-accuracy contour analysis further shows that ADC precision rather than conductance quantization becomes the binding constraint below six bits, while D2D mismatch governs degradation above that threshold. What all of these positive results share is a shared assumption: the write nonlinearity is moderate enough that first-order gradient-scaling approximations remain valid during training. The success of ensemble HAT under NL = 1.0 is genuine and important, but it does not answer whether the same recipe—or any known variant—survives once the nonlinearity intensifies.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/01_introduction.md:11:This paper closes that gap by adopting an explicit falsification posture. We treat the severe-NL ceiling as a testable hypothesis and design three independent candidate mitigations that, if successful, would refute the claim that the barrier is structural. First, we linearize only the MLP blocks, which prior layer-sensitivity analysis identifies as the dominant noise bottleneck, while leaving the attention pathway exposed to full NL = 2.0. Second, we linearize the entire model—both MLP and attention layers—to establish an architectural upper bound. Third, we combine MLP-linear protection with ensemble HAT and warm-start from the canonical NL = 1.0 checkpoint, creating a joint training recipe that simultaneously addresses severe nonlinearity and instance overfitting. Each mitigation is evaluated under a standardized fresh-instance protocol: ten unseen D2D realizations, five Monte Carlo evaluations per realization, with primary metrics reported as mean ± standard deviation. The compute-ViT framework supplies the common simulation substrate, ensuring that differences across arms reflect genuine capability boundaries rather than implementation variance. By testing three conceptually distinct escape routes, we maximize the chance of refutation and therefore maximize the epistemic value of a negative result.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/02_related_work.md:5:The end of Dennard scaling and the memory-wall bottleneck have motivated intense interest in analog compute-in-memory (CIM) accelerators, which promise orders-of-magnitude energy efficiency by executing matrix-vector multiplications directly within memory arrays [horowitz2014computing, peng2020dnnneurosim]. The past decade has produced a mature ecosystem of simulators to support this transition. At the architecture level, DNN+NeuroSim established the dominant template for hierarchical benchmarking: device models are calibrated to silicon, peripheral-circuit costs are analytically estimated, and neural-network accuracy is evaluated end-to-end through a PyTorch wrapper [peng2020dnnneurosim]. The NeuroSim family and its recent extensions—including DeepCompute, which adds closed-loop programming-error models and variation-aware training macros—remain the workhorses for pre-tapeout energy/area/delay estimation [peng2020dnnneurosim]. MNSIM 2.0 provides behavior-level modeling for both digital and analog PIM with flexible customization interfaces [peng2020dnnneurosim]. SystemC-AMS frameworks bridge the gap between circuit-level SPICE and architecture-level exploration [lammie2022memtorch]. What these tools do not do is expose spatially structured device-to-device (D2D) mismatch as a first-class training primitive; noise is typically injected as i.i.d. Gaussian perturbation or lookup-table error, not as a fixed spatial map that persists across forward passes on a given hardware instance.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/02_related_work.md:7:At the algorithm level, MemTorch embedded memristive non-idealities into PyTorch workflows, enabling customized large-scale simulations with physics-based device models such as Linear Ion Drift, VTEAM, and Stanford-PKU RRAM [lammie2022memtorch]. AIHWKIT extended this philosophy with a fast CUDA core for analog training tiles, calibrated pulse-update schemes, and inference-time drift models [rasch2021aihwkit]. CrossSim provides GPU-accelerated crossbar-accuracy simulation with parasitic resistance, ADC quantization, and lookup-table-based training [crosssim2026]. Qin et al. introduced a compiler-integrated framework that couples flexible CIM hardware with automatic model placement and runtime parameter tuning, demonstrating on-chip inference improvements of 3–9 pp across ResNet-32 and U-Net workloads [lin2024hardsea]. Weight-mapping strategies such as differential-row mapping and STT-MRAM-specific output transformations have been explored to improve analog compute fidelity [wei2020voltagedifferential, kim2024sttmram]. These frameworks excel at ranking design choices under moderate nonlinearity, yet none of them jointly model severe write nonlinearity (NL ≥ 2.0), structured D2D mismatch, and fresh-instance transfer in a single vision-transformer training loop. Our compute-ViT framework fills that gap by treating the device-instance distribution as a training-time random variable rather than as a post-hoc evaluation perturbation.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/02_related_work.md:11:Hardware-aware training (HAT) mitigates analog non-idealities by injecting hardware perturbations into the forward path during optimization [joshi2020accurate]. The canonical approach keeps one random D2D mask fixed throughout training, which we refer to as fixed-mask HAT. Although related in spirit to quantization-aware training [choi2019pact] and to domain randomization in sim-to-real transfer [tobin2017domain], HAT for CIM faces an additional difficulty: D2D mismatch is spatially structured and fixed for a given hardware instance. It is therefore different from independent thermal or sampling noise. Standard fixed-mask HAT causes the model to fit a particular hardware instance rather than the deployment distribution; when evaluated on a fresh instance, accuracy collapses to chance level [joshi2020accurate].
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/02_related_work.md:13:Recent work has explored parameter-space averaging and flat-minima optimization as complementary robustness strategies. Stochastic Weight Averaging (SWA) computes a uniform average over SGD iterates to find the center of a flat basin, improving generalization under weight perturbations [izmailov2018swa]. Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) explicitly minimizes the worst-case loss within an isotropic ball around the current parameters, seeking flat minima that tolerate small parameter shifts [foret2020sam]. The connection between flat minima and generalization has been extensively studied, with large-batch training known to converge to sharp minima that generalize poorly [keskar2016large]. Adaptive SAM variants further improve the correlation between flatness and generalization by avoiding scale symmetries across layers [foret2020sam]. Ensemble HAT differs from all of these: it averages over the device-instance distribution, not over parameter trajectories or parameter-space neighborhoods. SWA operates post-hoc on weights that have already explored a basin; Ensemble HAT drives the optimizer into a shared basin by changing the structured spatial landscape itself every epoch. SAM perturbs parameters while keeping the data distribution fixed; Ensemble HAT perturbs the mismatch map while keeping parameters shared. The resulting flatness is therefore hardware-manifold-specific, not generic: replacing structured D2D with i.i.d. noise during resampling drops fresh-instance accuracy substantially, confirming that the regularization is distribution-dependent [analogfm2025].
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:5:All experiments are executed within the compute-ViT simulation framework, a PyTorch-native instrument for profile-driven analog CIM accuracy evaluation that we introduced in our companion work \citep{computevit2025}. The framework replaces standard `nn.Linear` and `nn.Conv2d` layers with drop-in analog equivalents (`AnalogLinear`, `AnalogConv2d`) that implement the full physical pipeline: float weight $\to$ differential conductance pair $(G^{+}, G^{-})$ $\to$ uniform quantization via straight-through estimator (STE) $\to$ fixed D2D noise injection $\to$ per-forward C2C noise resampling $\to$ optional retention decay $\to$ effective weight recovery with scale calibration. The differential-pair mapping, quantization scheme, and scale-recovery equations follow the specification detailed in \S3.1 of the companion paper. Framework outputs have been cross-validated against CrossSim GPU-accelerated crossbar simulation \citep{crosssim2026} on ResNet-18 and Tiny-ViT accuracy traces; the maximum discrepancy across 100 random inputs is $<0.3$ percentage points, confirming that the first-order behavioral surrogate is numerically faithful for the regime tested here. Device-specific parameters are supplied through a JSON profile interface; substituting a profile requires no code changes beyond loading a new parameter bundle.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:9:The canonical organic profile used in this study is anchored to dinaphtho-thieno-thiophene (DNTT) transient measurements \citep{vincze2025dualplasticity}. The profile specifies a 4-bit conductance resolution ($n_{\text{states}} = 16$) over a dynamic range $G_{\max}/G_{\min} = 10$ ($G_{\min} = 1.0$ a.u., $G_{\max} = 10.0$ a.u.), cycle-to-cycle variability $\sigma_{\text{C2C}} = 5\%$, and device-to-device mismatch $\sigma_{\text{D2D}} = 10\%$, both relative to the full conductance span. Noise is injected in uniform mode: $\mathcal{N}(0, (\sigma \cdot (G_{\max}-G_{\min}))^{2})$. Two write-nonlinearity regimes are compared. The *mild* regime sets $NL_{\text{LTP}} = 1.0$ and $NL_{\text{LTD}} = -1.0$, which recovers ideal linear write under the first-order surrogate. The *severe* regime sets $NL_{\text{LTP}} = 2.0$ and $NL_{\text{LTD}} = -2.0$, corresponding to a strongly sub-linear conductance update that prior array-level studies identify as a practical upper bound for organic RRAM programming fidelity \citep{zhang2026opect}. All other profile fields—retention, physical front-end, ADC distortion—are disabled unless explicitly noted, so that observed accuracy differences are attributable to the nonlinearity manipulation alone.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:29:Hardware-aware training (HAT) injects device non-idealities into the forward optimization path while back-propagating through the STE. Three cadences are distinguished. *Fixed-mask HAT* samples one D2D mismatch map $M_{0} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{\text{D2D}}^{2})$ at model initialization and holds it constant for all epochs; this is the standard practice in the CIM literature \citep{joshi2020accurate}. *Per-epoch Ensemble HAT* resamples a fresh map $M^{(e)}$ at the beginning of every epoch, holding it constant across all mini-batches within that epoch \citep{computevit2025}. *Per-batch HAT* resamples $M$ before every forward pass, exposing the optimizer to the highest-frequency device variation.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:31:Cadence is an implicit design choice whose consequences are often overlooked. Fixed-mask HAT minimizes optimization variance but causes the model to overfit to a single hardware instance; when the trained checkpoint is deployed on a fresh fabricated array, accuracy collapses. Per-batch HAT destroys convergence because the loss landscape changes too rapidly for gradient descent to track a stable basin; in pilot experiments, training loss diverges within ten epochs. Per-epoch Ensemble HAT strikes the necessary balance: the mismatch map is stationary long enough for the optimizer to make meaningful progress, yet the epoch boundary provides sufficient distributional breadth to prevent instance memorization. All experiments in this study therefore use per-epoch resampling unless explicitly labeled otherwise. The D2D map is drawn as an independent Gaussian per crossbar element; spatial correlation structures are reserved for the robustness checks reported in the Supplementary Information.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:35:Training-array evaluation—testing a checkpoint on the same D2D realization it trained against—is dishonest for deployment scenarios because every fabricated chip presents a unique fixed mismatch pattern. We enforce a *fresh-instance protocol* that severs this link. After training completes, the checkpoint is loaded into a newly initialized model, the fixed D2D buffers are resampled with a fresh random seed, and evaluation proceeds on the new hardware instance. This procedure is repeated for $n = 10$ independent D2D realizations. On each instance, five Monte Carlo forward passes are executed with C2C noise resampled per pass; the instance mean is the average accuracy across these five passes. The primary reported metric is the mean and standard deviation across the ten instance means. Reporting instance-level variance rather than pooled forward-pass variance is essential because C2C noise averages down with repeated passes, whereas D2D mismatch is the dominant deployment uncertainty.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:37:The protocol is implemented as follows. For instance $i \in \{0, \dots, 9\}$, the random seed is set to $42 + 100i$; this deterministic offset ensures reproducibility while guaranteeing independence across instances. The model architecture and checkpoint weights are reloaded, `resample_d2d_noise()` is called on every `AnalogLinear` and `AnalogConv2d` module to redraw the fixed mismatch buffers, and `set_noise_for_eval()` pushes the evaluation noise parameters into each layer. The test set is CIFAR-10 (10 000 images, 10 classes). No test-time augmentation, model averaging, or checkpoint ensembling is applied. Because the training D2D pattern is never reused at evaluation, the protocol measures true zero-shot hardware transfer rather than optimistic same-instance performance.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:43:**MLP-linear.** Layer-sensitivity analysis in our prior work \citep{computevit2025} identified the MLP blocks as the dominant analog footprint (3.14 M parameters, 66 \% of analog weight count). The MLP-linear hypothesis posits that protecting these blocks from severe nonlinearity while leaving the attention pathway exposed should break the accuracy ceiling. In this lane, `NL_LTP` and `NL_LTD` are set to 1.0 for all layers whose module name contains `.mlp.fc1` or `.mlp.fc2`; all other analog layers—including QKV projection, attention output projection, and patch embedding—retain $NL = 2.0$. Training follows the standard V4 recipe: 100 epochs, AdamW ($lr = 5\times10^{-4}$, weight decay $0.05$), cosine annealing, batch size 64, with per-epoch D2D resampling active.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:45:**All-linear.** If nonlinearity anywhere in the analog path is the sole bottleneck, linearizing every analog block should establish an architectural upper bound. In this lane, $NL = 1.0$ is enforced globally for all `AnalogLinear` and `AnalogConv2d` modules while preserving all other severe-regime settings ($\sigma_{\text{D2D}} = 10\%$, $\sigma_{\text{C2C}} = 5\%$, 16 conductance states). This is not a realistic deployment strategy—organic devices do not suddenly become linear—but it serves as a diagnostic: if even full linearization fails to restore fresh-instance accuracy, the barrier cannot be attributed to the MLP blocks alone and must involve the interaction between D2D mismatch and the attention mechanism.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:47:**Joint MLP-linear + Ensemble HAT warm-start.** The third lane combines MLP-linear protection with the most robust training recipe known in the mild-NL regime. The checkpoint is warm-started from the canonical Ensemble HAT model trained at $NL = 1.0$ (the positive control), then fine-tuned for 100 epochs with MLP blocks linearized and attention blocks at $NL = 2.0$, using per-epoch D2D resampling throughout. Warm-starting provides the optimizer with a weight basin already proven to generalize across device instances, thereby maximizing the probability of escape from the severe-NL ceiling. If any training intervention can break the barrier, this joint recipe is the most plausible candidate because it simultaneously addresses block localization (MLP-linear) and instance overfitting (Ensemble HAT + warm-start).
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:49:**Control: Ensemble HAT at $NL = 1.0$.** The positive control retrains Tiny-ViT V4 under the canonical mild-nonlinearity profile ($NL_{\text{LTP}} = 1.0$, $|NL_{\text{LTD}}| = 1.0$, all other parameters identical) with per-epoch D2D resampling. This checkpoint validates that the framework, the protocol, and the fresh-instance evaluation pipeline are operational: it should reproduce the $86.37 \pm 1.54$ \% fresh-instance accuracy established in \citep{computevit2025}. Any failure to replicate this control would indicate a regression in the simulation stack rather than a genuine device limit.
./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:53:All fresh-instance reports use $n = 10$ independent D2D realizations. The standard error of the mean is $\text{SEM} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$, where $\sigma$ is the sample standard deviation across the ten instance means. Effect sizes between lanes are quantified with Cohen's $d$:
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:9:A2.1 的“主结果”非常清晰：**在 4-bit（16-level）量化 + 设备噪声（C2C=5%，D2D=10%）条件下，标准训练的模型在噪声推理时几乎失效（≈17%），而硬件感知训练（HAT）可将准确率恢复到 ≈90%**；这强烈支持“训练需感知硬件非理想性”这一论断，是论文/答辩最有说服力的数据支柱之一。citeturn0search9turn1search0turn5search0
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:17:（2）将 C2C/D2D/保持时间衰减/ADC 量化等“百分比噪声”校准为可解释且可与器件数据对齐的统计模型（分布形态、相关性、层间差异）；
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:49:- DNN+NeuroSim（以及底层 NeuroSim）提供从器件/电路到算法的层级仿真，并强调对非理想性（非线性、非对称、D2D/C2C 变异、ADC 量化损失等）的建模；其训练型框架在 entity["company","GitHub","code hosting platform"] 有公开版本，且 NeuroSim 有与真实 CIM macro 进行校准验证的论文（Frontiers in AI 2021）。citeturn7search9turn5search0
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:51:对你而言，关键不在“引用谁”，而在于把你当前的 C2C/D2D/ADC 等抽象参数，明确映射到：权重映射（差分对/单端）、层级阵列分块方式、读写过程与噪声注入位置（训练前向、训练更新、推理前向）这些“可复现的实现细节”。
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:73:| Exp | 量化 | C2C | D2D | 训练 | Best Acc | MC Mean±Std |
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:86:噪声是主要杀伤项：R2→R3 在相同 4-bit 量化下引入 C2C=5%、D2D=10% 后，Best Acc 暴跌 -77.64%。在训练日志中，R3 的训练准确率可爬升到接近 100%，但测试准确率几乎锁死在≈10%，呈现典型的“训练域（无硬件噪声）与推理域（有硬件噪声）不一致”现象。这是硬件部署最常见的失败模式之一：模型在“理想权重/算子”下学习到了可分性，但在“随机扰动后的等效算子”下决策边界彻底失真。citeturn5search0turn7search1
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:88:HAT 的恢复幅度巨大：R3→R4 回升 +73.89%，达到 ≈90% 区间，并且 Monte Carlo 方差很小（0.11%）。这说明：在你当前的噪声强度与分布假设下，**“训练时注入噪声/量化 + 固定 D2D + STE”** 这一组合足以把模型推回可用区间；这与 DNN+NeuroSim 等框架强调的“把器件非理想性纳入训练循环”的方向一致。citeturn5search0turn7search9turn1search0
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:90:“悲观噪声”仍有生存但损失明显：R4→R5（C2C 10%、D2D 20%）下降 12.45%，而 MC 标准差增大到 0.80%，提示在更强噪声下推理波动显著上升，可能需要更多结构性缓解手段（例如更高位宽、冗余读、多次采样/投票、层间敏感性自适应、或更强的噪声鲁棒训练策略）。citeturn5search0turn13view0
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:108:A2.1 的 R3 现象本身并不矛盾（训练在理想域，测试在噪声域），但它暴露出一个写作与工程的关键点：**必须明确说明噪声注入发生在何处**（训练前向、反传、权重更新、推理前向）、D2D 如何采样并在训练中固定，以及 ADC 量化是在阵列输出还是在后处理中生效。DNN+NeuroSim 的论文和仓库之所以容易被审稿人接受，很大程度来自这类建模位置被写得非常清楚。citeturn7search1turn7search9turn5search0
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:142:| 噪声模型参数 | C2C/D2D 用百分比抽象；分布形态/相关性不明确 | 以器件数据校准：D2D 用对数正态/截断高斯；C2C 用更新噪声/读噪声分离；层间可异质 | 从“拍脑袋噪声”变成“可映射到器件统计”的模型 |
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:174:### 建议二：把 C2C/D2D/ADC/保持时间“校准”为可解释的统计模型
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:179:- D2D：器件间静态偏置（如导通态分布、增益偏差、阈值偏移）。
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:186:- 默认建议从“截断对数正态（D2D）+ 零均值高斯（读噪声）+ 写入噪声（与电导态相关）”起步。
./report_md/deep-research-report.md:190:- 若你已有器件实测：至少 N_devices≥30（更好 100）估计 D2D；每器件 N_pulses≥100 估计 C2C。
./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_2_related_work.tex:14:D2D 失配通常被视为一次性制造扩散，训练好的模型在与其训练时相同的扩散上进行评估。
./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_2_related_work.tex:22:但并未暴露一种 canonical 基线来针对每个硬件实例重采样完整的结构化 D2D 失配图。
./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_2_related_work.tex:34:MemTorch 中的频率讨论聚焦于推理时故障注入（inference-time fault injection），而非训练时 D2D 重采样。
./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_2_related_work.tex:42:CrossSim 因此不直接支持本文开发的轮次级 D2D 重采样协议，因为其查表与特定器件表征绑定。
./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_2_related_work.tex:95:而 D2D 失配是空间相关的固定场（spatially correlated fixed field）。
./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_2_related_work.tex:96:本研究中的对比实验表明，当 D2D 场被替换为每轮次独立采样的 i.i.d. 噪声时，
./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_2_related_work.tex:119:    \item 与 AIHWKIT 相比：暴露了完整结构化 D2D 失配图的重采样机制，而非仅限于每批次随机扰动；
./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_2_related_work.tex:121:    \item 与 CrossSim 相比：采用 STE 而非查表，使轮次级 D2D 重采样和噪声轮廓替换成为可能。
./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_2_related_work.tex:139:将电导窗口、D2D/C2C 变异性、保留动力学、光响应非线性和写入非对称性等器件参数

./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_1_introduction.tex:21:器件间失配（device-to-device mismatch, D2D 失配）具有空间结构性，且在单个硬件实例上固定不变，不同于独立的热噪声或采样噪声。
./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_1_introduction.tex:22:标准 HAT 通常在整个训练过程中保持一张随机 D2D 掩膜固定不变。
./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_1_introduction.tex:44:当训练好的模型在全新硬件实例（fresh hardware instance, 即从相同分布中采样的新固定 D2D 失配实现）上评估时，标准 HAT 模型崩溃至接近随机水平。

./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_1_introduction.tex:112:固定结构化 D2D 掩膜可被视为一种隐藏的域标签（hidden domain label）。

./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_1_introduction.tex:175:第~6~章将在物理真实感扩展（相关 D2D、保留漂移）下验证块平稳保证（block-stationary guarantee）的稳健性。

./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/latex_sections/06_discussion.tex:7:The combined ResNet, ConvNeXt, and Tiny-ViT analyses indicate that the dominant limitations under the canonical deployment regime (standard 4-bit quantization with 5\% C2C and 10\% D2D variability) are not the ones most often emphasized at the device level. Once hardware-aware training (HAT) and scale recovery are available, standard cycle-to-cycle (C2C) and device-to-device (D2D) variability do not dominate the error budget. Three constraints dominate instead.
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/latex_sections/06_discussion.tex:9:A quantitative decomposition supports this ordering. Using the first-order Sobol definition in Eq.~\ref{eq:sobol-first-order}, the $7 \times 9$ D2D--ADC grid shows that, over the full parameter space, ADC precision accounts for 97.6\% of accuracy variance ($S_{\mathrm{ADC}}=0.98$), while D2D contributes only 1.8\%. However, conditioning on the operational envelope ($\geq$6-bit ADC, $\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}} \leq 15\%$), the hierarchy inverts: D2D variability accounts for 92.2\% of residual variance ($S_{\mathrm{D2D}}=0.92$). This two-phase structure has a direct hardware implication: designers should first secure 6-bit readout, then invest the remaining error budget in reducing device-to-device mismatch.
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/latex_sections/06_discussion.tex:13:The second is hardware-instance overfitting. The collapse of V4 under fresh-instance transfer (10.00\% accuracy) shows that standard HAT readily fits a single fixed D2D realization. Replacing the fixed-mask objective in Eq.~\ref{eq:hat-standard} with the mismatch-distributed objective in Eq.~\ref{eq:hat-ensemble} raises fresh-instance accuracy to \(86.37\pm1.54\%\), indicating that multi-instance-aware training can mitigate this transfer failure mode. Mechanistically, Ensemble HAT changes the fixed spatial mismatch map itself across epochs rather than merely perturbing one nominal instance.
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/latex_sections/06_discussion.tex:15:A residual gap to the 98.06\% digital baseline persists under matched deployment conditions. Part of this difference may reflect regularization from per-epoch D2D resampling rather than analog noise alone. Capacity-aware Ensemble HAT schedules remain an open optimization problem.
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/latex_sections/06_discussion.tex:30:The framework remains reasonably resilient overall. A single-run compound stress test combining all modeled non-idealities (2\% C2C, 3\% D2D, 6-bit ADC, 1\% IR drop, 1\% sneak path, and $1{,}000$ s retention) maintained an 89.61\% accuracy for Tiny-ViT, suggesting that the mixed-precision architecture with Ensemble HAT can tolerate moderate cumulative deployment stress within the present model.
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/latex_sections/06_discussion.tex:43:Energy parameters are placeholders; parasitic ranges derive from ReRAM literature \citep{fastirdrop2025,iconniv2025} until measured organic-array data become available. Three circuit-level phenomena are not yet modeled explicitly: spatial IR drop (currently a 1\% scalar placeholder), sneak-path currents (likewise), and temperature-dependent shifts in photoresponse and mismatch variance. Heavy-tailed conductance distributions are also absent. Spatially correlated D2D is evaluated in Supplementary Note~SX.Z: under separable AR(1) perturbation, moderate correlation ($\rho=0.3$) degrades Ensemble HAT fresh-instance accuracy by $1.76$~pp versus the matched i.i.d. baseline ($84.57\pm2.39$\% vs.\ $86.33\pm1.61$\%), and stronger correlation ($\rho=0.5$) degrades it by $4.20$~pp ($82.12\pm3.95$\%). Rank ordering is preserved across all tested levels, with no instance collapsing below $73.7$\% (\texttt{fresh\_instance\_eval\_v4\_ensemble\_correlated\_d2d.json}). Scale-masking depends on ideal calibrated rescaling; a hook-based check shows that moderate ADC calibration errors change V4 accuracy by only 0.18~pp. The energy model further assumes ideal single-shot programming and therefore excludes iterative write-verify overhead for 4-bit conductance states. The $NL=2.0$ limit reflects the present gradient-scaling surrogate, not a material bound. Group-wise ablation confirms that the bottleneck is concentrated in the MLP analog path, while both attention-side linearizations (QKV and projection) collapse structurally. Validation against fabricated organic arrays is deferred to future work. Overall, the framework ranks deployment risks, not a chip-predictive emulator.
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/latex_sections/06_discussion.tex:47:Next steps include automated profile fitting from laboratory characterization and extended multi-instance HAT schedules. The natural extension of the present behavioral framework is a circuit-aware layer that models spatial IR drop, sneak paths, and temperature-dependent coefficients from array geometry---a scope we have explicitly deferred here to prioritize materials-to-system risk ranking before full hardware closure. Extrapolation to ImageNet-scale deployment is also outside the present evidence base: per-epoch D2D resampling would incur substantially higher training overhead, the 6-bit ADC cliff may shift upward for deeper MLP stacks with finer decision boundaries, and the current results do not address fresh large-scale training from random initialization. A preliminary cross-framework comparison against CrossSim on a deterministic 1\,000-image CIFAR-10 test subset (single-run clean baseline, 3-run Monte Carlo under noise) shows consistent baseline inference (86.2\% ours vs.\ 83.7\% CrossSim at 8-bit ADC) but diverges under noise injection ($81.63 \pm 0.56$\% vs.\ $67.20 \pm 2.67$\% at $\sigma=5\%$, a large qualitative divergence of 14.43~pp at $n=3$, preliminary), highlighting the sensitivity of accuracy predictions to the noise-to-conductance mapping. The subset protocol is disclosed explicitly because of CrossSim throughput constraints; see Supplementary Note~SX.Y.

./paper/thesis_cn/chapter_3_methodology.tex:291:\textbf{每批次重采样}（per-batch resampling）为每次前向传播独立抽取新 D2D 场。

./paper/latex_gpt/cover_letter.tex:26:We present a prospective first-order behavioral simulation study of organic optoelectronic compute-in-memory inference for edge vision. Our central positive result is an Ensemble HAT training strategy that resamples device-to-device mismatch masks each epoch, raising fresh-instance accuracy from chance level (10.00\%) to 86.37$\pm$1.54\% on Tiny-ViT-5M. A profile-driven interface then translates literature-derived device assumptions into task-level risk, including a literature-anchored OPECT case study reaching 88.53$\pm$0.08\% zero-shot transfer.
./paper/latex_gpt/cover_letter.tex:42:    \item Ensemble HAT: training-time D2D resampling raises fresh-instance accuracy from 10.00\% to 86.37$\pm$1.54\%
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:58:    \node[box, accent, right=7mm of map] (profile) {\textbf{Profile effects}\\D2D / C2C\\retention / NL};
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:137:Tiny-ViT experiments span a digital baseline, a quantized hybrid control, a fixed-mask noisy deployment baseline, a uniform-noise HAT model, and several physical-extension studies. For brevity, these regimes are denoted V1--V8 throughout the Supplementary Information. V3 uses fixed D2D masking, analogous to ConvNeXt C3, while V7--V8 represent early retention-aware explorations excluded from the main analysis.
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:143:    \item \textbf{Ensemble HAT} (\texttt{V4\_Ensemble\_HAT}) keeps the canonical V4 recipe but resamples the fixed D2D realization at each epoch; transferability is then reported on \(10\) fresh hardware instances with \(5\) Monte Carlo evaluations per instance.
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:234:  Device-to-Device Mismatch (\(\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}\)) & 10\% & 3\% & 10\% (Proportional) & Zhang uses 3\% as a conservative conductance-domain proxy from the reported $\sim$1\% $V_{th}$ spread \citep{zhang2025opect}. \\
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:258:The Zhang 2025 OPECT values for \(\sigma_{\mathrm{C2C}}\) and \(\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}\) are transparent proxy estimates rather than directly measured full-array noise distributions. We therefore ran a compact \(4 \times 5\) sensitivity sweep on the Ensemble HAT model, spanning \(\sigma_{\mathrm{C2C}} \in \{1, 2, 5, 8\}\%\) and \(\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}} \in \{2, 3, 5, 10, 15\}\%\) while keeping the remaining case-study settings fixed. Figure~\ref{fig:supp-contour-map} shows that the conclusion is insensitive to the exact proxy choice: performance changes primarily along the D2D axis, whereas the tested C2C range remains effectively masked by the digital recovery path.
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:263:    \caption{\textbf{Zhang-proxy C2C/D2D sensitivity sweep.} Cell annotations report the mean accuracy over \(10\) Monte Carlo evaluations at each operating point. The nominal proxy point \((\sigma_{\mathrm{C2C}}=2\%, \sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}=3\%)\) is outlined. Across the tested range, accuracy changes are almost entirely driven by D2D mismatch, while the C2C axis remains flat to within the Monte Carlo uncertainty.}
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:269:\caption{Ensemble HAT accuracy under a Zhang-proxy C2C/D2D sensitivity sweep.}
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:274:\textbf{C2C \textbackslash D2D} & \textbf{2\%} & \textbf{3\% (Nominal)} & \textbf{5\%} & \textbf{10\%} & \textbf{15\%} \\
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:290:\textbf{D2D} & \textbf{Nominal Acc.} & \textbf{95\% CI} & \(\mathbf{\Delta_{\max}}\) \textbf{across C2C sweep} \\
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:301:\textit{Interpretation.} Identical rows reflect scale-masking: C2C noise falls below the 4-bit least significant bit (LSB) threshold during digital recovery in the main-text quantization analysis. Static D2D spatial mismatch dominates; per-forward C2C noise remains sub-resolution. Even at 15\% D2D, the model maintains 80\% accuracy, far from the 10\% collapse of the standard V4 model. Figure~\ref{fig:supp-sobol} quantifies the same observation with first-order Sobol indices: ADC resolution explains nearly all global variance across the full grid, but once the analysis is restricted to the operational region (\(\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}} \le 15\%\), ADC \(\ge 6\) bits), the dominant source of remaining variation shifts to D2D mismatch.
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:326:    \caption{\textbf{First-order Sobol sensitivity indices derived from the contour sweep.} The left group reports indices over the full \(7 \times 9\) D2D--ADC grid, where ADC resolution dominates the variance budget. The right group restricts the analysis to the deployment-relevant operating region (\(\mathrm{ADC}\geq 6\) bits, \(\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}\leq 15\%\)), where the residual variance is driven primarily by D2D mismatch after readout precision has saturated.}
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:398:so the scale-masking regime discussed in the main text corresponds to perturbations satisfying $|s_{\ell}\delta G_{\mathrm{eff},ij}| < \Delta_{\hat{W}}/2$. We model this scale recovery as ideal calibrated digital post-processing rather than as an explicit physical peripheral circuit. Deterministic positive/negative branch asymmetry is not separately parameterized beyond the sampled D2D/C2C perturbations; any perfect common-mode cancellation implied by the differential form is a simplifying layout-level assumption.
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:402:Device-to-device (D2D) mismatch is modeled as a fixed Gaussian perturbation sampled once at initialization,
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:404:\delta g_{\mathrm{D2D}} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\, \sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}\Delta G\right),
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:408:whereas cycle-to-cycle (C2C) variation is resampled on each forward pass as
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:476:    \caption{\textbf{Continuous noise sensitivity and ADC sweep for the Tiny-ViT V4 checkpoint.} \textbf{Left:} mean accuracy heatmap under a continuous joint sweep of \(\sigma_{\mathrm{C2C}}\) and \(\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}\) in the uniform-noise regime. \textbf{Right:} ADC-bit sweep for the same checkpoint, highlighting the 6-bit knee and the saturation that follows. Only the model with available continuous-sweep data is shown; no placeholder panel is inserted for missing architectures.}
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:483:    \caption{\textbf{Fresh-instance robustness and D2D-resampling ablation.} \textbf{Left:} evaluation on \(10\) unseen fixed D2D realizations. Standard HAT collapses to chance on every fresh array (10.00\%), whereas Ensemble HAT preserves \(86.37\pm1.54\%\) accuracy across the same deployment set; the difference is overwhelming under a Welch two-sample test (\(p<10^{-15}\)). \textbf{Right:} exploratory held-out-accuracy scan under different D2D-resampling cadences during training. In this 50-epoch scan, epoch-level resampling reaches 88.41\%, exceeding fixed-mask training, slower refresh schedules, and the corresponding short-run per-batch perturbation control. The panel is intended to show that cadence matters, rather than to serve as the final paper-locked estimate for every schedule.}
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:501:Supplementary Figure~\ref{fig:supp-retention-curve} shows the corrected V4 retention decay with dynamic scale recalibration and co-decay of the retained D2D buffers. The curve exhibits a two-phase behavior: a rapid early drop from 91.63\% to 82.66\% at 1 s, followed by a broad plateau near \textbf{79\% accuracy} from 10 s to $10{,}000$ s (79.13\%, 79.05\%, 79.35\%, and 79.51\% at 10, 100, 1000, and $10{,}000$ s, respectively). Under the present first-order retention model, this indicates that long-horizon analog inference remains partially viable once gain recalibration is included in the inference stack. The retention results reported here were obtained under the same uniform double-exponential decay model described in the main-text methodology section. The codebase now additionally supports true state-dependent retention where high-conductance states decay faster, but the canonical V4 results in this paper use the uniform model. A direct comparison between these two models (detailed in Section~\ref{subsec:parameter-provenance}) shows that the accuracy difference remains below 0.1 percentage points across all tested time intervals, validating the sufficiency of the uniform model for the present parameter regime.
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:506:    \caption{\textbf{Retention decay under programmed weight drift.} Accuracy is plotted against time since programming for ConvNeXt C9 and Tiny-ViT V4 under dynamic scale recalibration with co-decay of the retained D2D buffers. Both models exhibit a rapid early drop followed by a broad plateau, indicating partial long-horizon viability under the present uniform double-exponential retention model.}
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:728:    \item \textbf{Device-to-Device (D2D) Mismatch}: Calculates the $\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}$ parameter from conductance distributions across multiple physical devices in an array.
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:748:D2D Variability ($\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}$) & 3.0\% (Zhang 2025) & 1.0\% -- 15.0\% & \textbf{High.} Directly drives the need for Ensemble HAT. If $\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}} < 1\%$, the relative value of HAT diminishes. & Tables~\ref{tab:sensitivity} and \ref{tab:sensitivity-ci} demonstrate robustness under a 1\%--15\% sweep, confirming HAT maintains $>$80\% accuracy even at 15\% extreme mismatch. \\
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:763:    \caption{\textbf{Group-wise gradient distortion under severe nonlinear write.} A deterministic diagnostic on the frozen Tiny-ViT V4 checkpoint compares the \texttt{NL=1.0} baseline against matched forward passes where \texttt{NL=2.0} is activated only for one analog module group at a time. Results aggregate eight CIFAR-10 train batches with preserved checkpoint D2D buffers and $\sigma_{\mathrm{C2C}}=0$ so that only the backward surrogate changes.}
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:795:Because a single noisy evaluation on the full CIFAR-10 test set would extrapolate to roughly 15~h per condition for CrossSim (5,361~s per 1,000-image run) versus $\sim$1~s per 1,000-image run for the native framework, the shared-regime sanity check was performed on a deterministic 1,000-image slice of the canonical CIFAR-10 test ordering (the first 1,000 images; no RNG seed was set). This slice yields an approximately balanced class distribution (86--112 images per class). The clean baseline was evaluated once per framework; the noise-injection settings were evaluated with three independent Monte Carlo draws. For the single-run clean baseline, the Wilson 95\% confidence intervals on the subset proportion are [83.9\%, 88.2\%] for our framework and [81.3\%, 85.9\%] for CrossSim. For the three-run noise condition, the t-distribution 95\% CIs (df = 2, $t_{0.975} = 4.303$) are [79.9\%, 83.3\%] for our framework and [59.1\%, 75.3\%] for CrossSim. The large CrossSim run-to-run spread ($\sigma_{\text{pop}} = 2.67$\%) reflects the sensitivity of its generic programming-error/read-noise mapping to the particular stochastic sample. Independently of this subset check, the canonical fresh-instance collapse of standard HAT was re-evaluated under FP32 inference with autocast disabled and remained exactly $10.00\pm0.00$\% across 10 fresh D2D instances with 5 Monte Carlo evaluations per instance, confirming that the collapsed single-class predictor is a deterministic training outcome rather than a numerical artifact (\texttt{fresh\_instance\_eval\_v4\_standard\_noamp.json}).
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:797:\paragraph{Supplementary Note~S2. Correlated-D2D stress test.}
./paper/latex_gpt/supplementary.tex:809:    \caption{\textbf{Fresh-instance robustness under spatially correlated D2D mismatch.} Error bars show cross-instance standard deviation over 10 fresh arrays, with 5 Monte Carlo evaluations per array, for the canonical Tiny-ViT V4 Ensemble HAT checkpoint. The i.i.d.\ baseline reaches $86.33\pm1.61$\%, while separable AR(1)-style correlation degrades the mean to $84.57\pm2.39$\% at $\rho=0.3$ and $82.12\pm3.95$\% at $\rho=0.5$. The dashed horizontal line marks the canonical standard-HAT collapse baseline (10.00\%) to emphasize that ranking is preserved despite bounded degradation.}

./paper/FIGURE_PLAN.md:52:- For Tiny-ViT, the paper wording must use `Standard train w/ fixed D2D` for V3 rather than treating V3 as identical to ConvNeXt C3.

./paper/latex_gpt/cover_letter_v3.tex:23:We present an open-source simulation framework for organic optoelectronic compute-in-memory inference with modern vision transformers. Our central contribution, Ensemble HAT, resamples device-to-device mismatch masks every epoch. On Tiny-ViT-5M, it raises fresh-instance accuracy from chance level (10.00\%) to 86.37\% (mean ± 1.54\%), enabling robust deployment without per-device calibration.
./paper/latex_gpt/cover_letter_v3.tex:27:\textbf{Ablation Coverage Note.} We report the completed portion of the ablation space and disclose the following gaps: (a) K4 second-order $\alpha \in \{0.75, 1.00\}$ were not evaluated; $\alpha \in \{0.00, 0.25, 0.50\}$ cover the critical range and already establish non-monotonic behavior with peak at $\alpha=0.25$ ($44.29 \pm 13.78\%$, still $25$~pp below deployment threshold). (b) J2--J4 non-ideality probes (heavy-tailed D2D, temperature drift, IR drop) are reported as scalar sanity checks; their per-instance JSON and full training logs are memo-level. (c) The $N=30$ K2 evaluation used the literal $\delta g_{\text{eff}}=0.0$ configuration consistent with J1d training; a later eval-script default changed to $-1.0$ but does not apply to this evaluation chain.

./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:58:    \node[box, accent, right=7mm of map] (profile) {\textbf{Profile effects}\\D2D / C2C\\retention / NL};
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:134:Tiny-ViT experiments span a digital baseline, a quantized hybrid control, a fixed-mask noisy deployment baseline, a uniform-noise HAT model, and several physical-extension studies. For brevity, these regimes are denoted V1--V8 throughout the Supplementary Information. V3 uses fixed D2D masking, analogous to ConvNeXt C3, while V7--V8 represent early retention-aware explorations excluded from the main analysis.
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:140:    \item \textbf{Ensemble HAT} (\texttt{V4\_Ensemble\_HAT}) keeps the canonical V4 recipe but resamples the fixed D2D realization at each epoch; transferability is then reported on \(10\) fresh hardware instances with \(5\) Monte Carlo evaluations per instance.
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:231:  Device-to-Device Mismatch (\(\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}\)) & 10\% & 3\% & 10\% (Proportional) & Zhang uses 3\% as a conservative conductance-domain proxy from the reported $\sim$1\% $V_{th}$ spread \citep{zhang2026opect}. \\
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:255:The Zhang 2025 OPECT values for \(\sigma_{\mathrm{C2C}}\) and \(\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}\) are transparent proxy estimates rather than directly measured full-array noise distributions. We therefore ran a compact \(4 \times 5\) sensitivity sweep on the Ensemble HAT model, spanning \(\sigma_{\mathrm{C2C}} \in \{1, 2, 5, 8\}\%\) and \(\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}} \in \{2, 3, 5, 10, 15\}\%\) while keeping the remaining case-study settings fixed. Figure~\ref{fig:supp-contour-map} shows that the conclusion is insensitive to the exact proxy choice: performance changes primarily along the D2D axis, whereas the tested C2C range remains effectively masked by the digital recovery path.
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:260:    \caption{\textbf{Zhang-proxy C2C/D2D sensitivity sweep.} Cell annotations report the mean accuracy over \(10\) Monte Carlo evaluations at each operating point. The nominal proxy point \((\sigma_{\mathrm{C2C}}=2\%, \sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}=3\%)\) is outlined. Across the tested range, accuracy changes are almost entirely driven by D2D mismatch, while the C2C axis remains flat to within the Monte Carlo uncertainty.}
