The panel is intended to show that cadence matters, rather than to serve as the final paper-locked estimate for every schedule.}
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:498:Supplementary Figure~\ref{fig:supp-retention-curve} shows the corrected V4 retention decay with dynamic scale recalibration and co-decay of the retained D2D buffers. The curve exhibits a two-phase behavior: a rapid early drop from 91.63\% to 82.66\% at 1 s, followed by a broad plateau near \textbf{79\% accuracy} from 10 s to $10{,}000$ s (79.13\%, 79.05\%, 79.35\%, and 79.51\% at 10, 100, 1000, and $10{,}000$ s, respectively). Under the present first-order retention model, this indicates that long-horizon analog inference remains partially viable once gain recalibration is included in the inference stack. The retention results reported here were obtained under the same uniform double-exponential decay model described in the main-text methodology section. The codebase now additionally supports true state-dependent retention where high-conductance states decay faster, but the canonical V4 results in this paper use the uniform model. A direct comparison between these two models (detailed in Section~\ref{subsec:parameter-provenance}) shows that the accuracy difference remains below 0.1 percentage points across all tested time intervals, validating the sufficiency of the uniform model for the present parameter regime.
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:503:    \caption{\textbf{Retention decay under programmed weight drift.} Accuracy is plotted against time since programming for ConvNeXt C9 and Tiny-ViT V4 under dynamic scale recalibration with co-decay of the retained D2D buffers. Both models exhibit a rapid early drop followed by a broad plateau, indicating partial long-horizon viability under the present uniform double-exponential retention model.}
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:725:    \item \textbf{Device-to-Device (D2D) Mismatch}: Calculates the $\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}$ parameter from conductance distributions across multiple physical devices in an array.
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:745:D2D Variability ($\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}$) & 3.0\% (Zhang 2025) & 1.0\% -- 15.0\% & \textbf{High.} Directly drives the need for Ensemble HAT. If $\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}} < 1\%$, the relative value of HAT diminishes. & Tables~\ref{tab:sensitivity} and \ref{tab:sensitivity-ci} demonstrate robustness under a 1\%--15\% sweep, confirming HAT maintains $>$80\% accuracy even at 15\% extreme mismatch. \\
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:760:    \caption{\textbf{Group-wise gradient distortion under severe nonlinear write.} A deterministic diagnostic on the frozen Tiny-ViT V4 checkpoint compares the \texttt{NL=1.0} baseline against matched forward passes where \texttt{NL=2.0} is activated only for one analog module group at a time. Results aggregate eight CIFAR-10 train batches with preserved checkpoint D2D buffers and $\sigma_{\mathrm{C2C}}=0$ so that only the backward surrogate changes.}
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:792:Because a single noisy evaluation on the full CIFAR-10 test set would extrapolate to roughly 15~h per condition for CrossSim (5,361~s per 1,000-image run) versus $\sim$1~s per 1,000-image run for the native framework, the shared-regime sanity check was performed on a deterministic 1,000-image slice of the canonical CIFAR-10 test ordering (the first 1,000 images; no RNG seed was set). This slice yields an approximately balanced class distribution (86--112 images per class). The clean baseline was evaluated once per framework; the noise-injection settings were evaluated with three independent Monte Carlo draws. For the single-run clean baseline, the Wilson 95\% confidence intervals on the subset proportion are [83.9\%, 88.2\%] for our framework and [81.3\%, 85.9\%] for CrossSim. For the three-run noise condition, the t-distribution 95\% CIs (df = 2, $t_{0.975} = 4.303$) are [79.9\%, 83.3\%] for our framework and [59.1\%, 75.3\%] for CrossSim. The large CrossSim run-to-run spread ($\sigma_{\text{pop}} = 2.67$\%) reflects the sensitivity of its generic programming-error/read-noise mapping to the particular stochastic sample. Independently of this subset check, the canonical fresh-instance collapse of standard HAT was re-evaluated under FP32 inference with autocast disabled and remained exactly $10.00\pm0.00$\% across 10 fresh D2D instances with 5 Monte Carlo evaluations per instance, confirming that the collapsed single-class predictor is a deterministic training outcome rather than a numerical artifact (\texttt{fresh\_instance\_eval\_v4\_standard\_noamp.json}).
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:794:\paragraph{Supplementary Note~SX.Z. Correlated-D2D stress test.}
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/code_snapshot/supplementary.tex:806:    \caption{\textbf{Fresh-instance robustness under spatially correlated D2D mismatch.} Error bars show cross-instance standard deviation over 10 fresh arrays, with 5 Monte Carlo evaluations per array, for the canonical Tiny-ViT V4 Ensemble HAT checkpoint. The i.i.d.\ baseline reaches $86.33\pm1.61$\%, while separable AR(1)-style correlation degrades the mean to $84.57\pm2.39$\% at $\rho=0.3$ and $82.12\pm3.95$\% at $\rho=0.5$. The dashed horizontal line marks the canonical standard-HAT collapse baseline (10.00\%) to emphasize that ranking is preserved despite bounded degradation.}
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/README.md:38:| `code_snapshot/scripts_gpt/eval_standard_fresh_instance_noamp.py` | Re-evaluates the paper-locked V4 checkpoint on 10 fresh D2D instances without AMP |
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/README.md:39:| `code_snapshot/scripts_gpt/eval_spatially_correlated_d2d.py` | Stress-test variant that injects spatially correlated D2D mismatch (AR(1) field) |
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/README.md:62:# 3. Evaluate across 10 fresh D2D instances to obtain the ensemble mean and std
./release_artifacts/zenodo_archive_v0/README.md:102:| Spatially correlated D2D stress test | `python code_snapshot/scripts_gpt/eval_spatially_correlated_d2d.py --rho 0.5` | Accuracy degradation curve vs. ρ | ~ 3 min |
./release_artifacts/source_data_v1/nl_gradient_distortion_pilot_gpt.json:8:    "checkpoint_path": "checkpoints/V4_hybrid_standard_noise_hat_best.pt",
./_archive/stale_json_20260419/_codex_auto_fitter_demo_fixed_20260408.json:16:    "NL_LTP": 1.50179868418594,
./_archive/stale_json_20260419/_codex_auto_fitter_demo_fixed_20260408.json:17:    "NL_LTD": -1.50179868418594,
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:40:The canonical hardware-aware training recipe fixes a single device-to-device (D2D) mismatch field $M_{0}$ for the entire training run. Under this fixed-mask objective, the optimizer solves
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:47:The empirical signature of this failure, documented exhaustively in Chapter~\ref{chap:failure-mode-atlas}, \S\ref{sec:failure-instance-overfitting}, is a deterministic collapse to chance level when the model is evaluated on fresh arrays. For Tiny-ViT V4 on CIFAR-10, the collapse is exact: $10.00\pm0.00\,\%$, the single-class baseline, across ten fresh D2D instances with five Monte Carlo evaluations each. A dedicated no-AMP control confirmed that the collapsed predictor is a deterministic training outcome, not a numerical artifact of mixed-precision autocast. The problem is therefore not a broken device or an unrealistic noise level; it is the \emph{training-time fixation of a random hardware sample} that the model subsequently memorizes.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:49:This memorization has a simple geometric interpretation. The D2D mismatch field $M_{0}$ is a spatially structured perturbation that acts as a hidden domain label. The classifier learns a decision boundary that is conditioned on $M_{0}$, much as a domain-adaptation network learns a boundary conditioned on a source domain. When the domain label changes at deployment, the boundary ceases to be valid. The $10.00\,\%$ collapse is the empirical cost of this domain shift.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:51:\subsection{Intervention: epoch-level D2D resampling}
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:53:Ensemble HAT replaces the single fixed mask with a distributional training objective in which one fresh D2D realization is drawn at the start of each epoch and held constant across all mini-batches within that epoch. Formally, the optimizer solves
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:62:The intervention does not require any change to the model architecture, the device assumptions, or the optimizer hyperparameters. It is a pure training-objective modification: the same Tiny-ViT backbone, the same 4-bit differential conductance mapping, the same AdamW schedule, but with the D2D field resampled at epoch boundaries. This minimalism is methodologically important. It means that the $10\,\%\rightarrow86\,\%$ lift is attributable solely to the distributional objective, not to extra capacity, extra training time, or a different physical model.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:77:The $86.37\pm1.54\,\%$ result is not the upper bound of what epoch-level resampling can achieve. Under the literature-calibrated organic phototransistor (OPECT) device profile ($\sigma_{\mathrm{C2C}}=2\,\%$, $\sigma_{\mathrm{D2D}}=3\,\%$), the same Ensemble HAT checkpoint reaches $88.53\pm0.08\,\%$ on fresh instances, demonstrating that the distributional objective generalizes to lower-noise profiles without retraining. Conversely, under spatially correlated D2D mismatch, the canonical Ensemble HAT checkpoint degrades gracefully: $86.33\pm1.61\,\%$ for i.i.d.~mismatch, $84.57\pm2.39\,\%$ at correlation strength $\rho=0.3$, and $82.12\pm3.95\,\%$ at $\rho=0.5$. These numbers are reproduced in Figure~\ref{fig:corr-d2d-c5}. The degradation is bounded and monotonic; no instance collapses below $73.7\,\%$, in stark contrast to the $10.00\,\%$ chance-level collapse of fixed-mask HAT. This suggests that the block-stationary training objective generalizes, at least qualitatively, beyond the i.i.d.~sampling law on which it was trained.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:82:    \caption{Fresh-instance robustness under spatially correlated D2D mismatch (reused from manuscript supplementary package, Fig.~S5). Error bars show cross-instance standard deviation over $10$ fresh arrays, with $5$ Monte Carlo evaluations per array, for the canonical Tiny-ViT V4 Ensemble HAT checkpoint.}
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:90:Ensemble HAT solves the i.i.d.~mismatch case, but three residual questions remain. First, does the block-stationary guarantee survive compound stress? The present experiments evaluate D2D mismatch, retention drift, nonlinear write, and front-end distortion in isolation; their joint statistics are unexplored. A model that tolerates each stress individually may still collapse when all are present simultaneously.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:92:Second, the correlated-D2D sweep uses a separable AR(1) proxy rather than a measured spatial covariance kernel. Real organic optoelectronic arrays may exhibit anisotropic correlation, heavy-tailed conductance distributions, or long-range wafer-scale gradients. Whether Ensemble HAT (or a correlated variant that resamples from the measured kernel) remains robust under these richer spatial structures is an open empirical question.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:94:Third, the $86.37\pm1.54\,\%$ result leaves a residual gap of approximately $11.6$~pp to the digital FP32 baseline of $98.06\,\%$. Part of this gap reflects the regularization cost of per-epoch D2D resampling, part reflects the 4-bit quantization floor, and part reflects the residual cycle-to-cycle (C2C) noise that is not averaged away by the distributional objective. Capacity-aware Ensemble HAT schedules---for example, varying the resampling frequency or noise amplitude as a function of training epoch---remain an open optimization problem.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:128:However, the fresh-instance evaluation tells a different story. When the MLP-only checkpoint is subjected to the standard $10\times5$ fresh-instance protocol, its accuracy drops to $32.12\pm7.72\,\%$. The mean is barely above the unmitigated baseline, and the standard deviation of $7.72$~pp is enormous compared to the $1.54$~pp spread of canonical Ensemble HAT. Some fresh instances are tolerable; others are catastrophic. This bimodal-like spread is qualitatively different from the tight distribution of Ensemble HAT and suggests that the MLP-linearized checkpoint has learned a \emph{fragile compensation} that is sensitive to the particular D2D pattern it encounters.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:153:A second open question concerns the large cross-instance variance. Does it reflect an instability that could be damped by larger mini-batches, longer training, or explicit variance-regularization penalties? Or does it reflect a fundamental sensitivity of the MLP-linearized geometry to the interaction between D2D mismatch and residual attention-side nonlinearity? The $7.72$~pp standard deviation is more than four times the canonical Ensemble HAT spread, and understanding its source would clarify whether the MLP-only lane is merely under-regularized or structurally non-robust.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:164:If severe nonlinear write is the dominant bottleneck, an obvious upper-bound experiment is to linearize \emph{all} analog-mapped layers---patch embedding, QKV projection, attention output projection, and both MLP feed-forward matrices---while keeping the rest of the training recipe unchanged. This ``all-linear'' lane removes the first-order gradient-scaling surrogate entirely from the analog stack, reducing the severe-NL problem to a pure D2D-mismatch problem under the canonical uniform-noise model. In theory, this should produce the best possible accuracy under the $NL=2.0$ training budget, because no analog layer suffers from write-asymmetry distortion.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:170:The intervention sets $NL_{\mathrm{LTP}}=1.0$ and $|NL_{\mathrm{LTD}}|=1.0$ for all analog-mapped layers during hardware-aware training. The D2D mismatch field remains fixed (this is the standard HAT protocol, not Ensemble HAT), and the C2C noise continues to resample per forward pass at $\sigma_{\mathrm{C2C}}=5\,\%$. The training hyperparameters are identical to the MLP-only lane: AdamW, learning rate $5\times10^{-4}$, weight decay $0.05$, cosine annealing, 100 epochs, batch size 64, 4-bit differential mapping.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:180:This result carries an important architectural implication. The small gap between MLP-only and all-linear source-domain accuracy ($0.30$~pp) means that the transformer can tolerate ideal attention projections without significant benefit. But the absence of any fresh-instance improvement means that the residual nonlinearity in attention is \emph{not} what prevents deployment-grade transfer. The bottleneck lies elsewhere: in the interaction between the D2D mismatch field and the severe-NL-compensated MLP geometry, or in the fact that both lanes were trained with a fixed D2D mask rather than with Ensemble HAT resampling.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:186:The central open question was whether the all-linear fresh-instance ceiling reflects a training-protocol deficiency (fixed-mask versus Ensemble HAT) or a deeper structural barrier. The CX-J1 joint-training experiment evaluated in \S\ref{sec:case-joint-hat} explicitly tests this hypothesis by combining MLP-linear compensation with epoch-level D2D resampling under severe NL. The result---$30.53\pm7.07\,\%$ fresh-instance accuracy, statistically indistinguishable from the fixed-mask all-linear ceiling of $32.60\pm9.18\,\%$---rejects the training-protocol explanation. Even with distributional training, the severe-NL regime imposes a $\sim$30\,\% ceiling. The bottleneck is therefore not the absence of D2D resampling; it is a structural property of the first-order NL surrogate acting on the attention pathway. What specific attention mechanism---QKV gradient asymmetry, output-projection scale recovery, or multi-head correlation---enforces the $\sim$30\,\% ceiling remains to be diagnosed.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:199:The MLP-only and all-linear case studies establish that partial or complete linearization of the analog stack under severe $NL=2.0$ recovers high source-domain accuracy ($87.79\,\%$ and $87.49\,\%$, respectively) but collapses to $\sim$32\,\% on fresh instances. A natural and optimistic hypothesis is that this $\sim$32\,\% ceiling is an \emph{optimization gap} rather than a structural barrier. Under this hypothesis, the failure is attributable to the training protocol---specifically, the use of a fixed D2D mask---and not to an inherent incompatibility between the first-order NL surrogate and the transformer attention pathway. If the hypothesis is correct, then adding Ensemble HAT's epoch-level D2D resampling to the MLP-linearized severe-NL recipe should regularize the optimizer sufficiently to break the ceiling and recover deployment-grade transfer.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:203:\subsection{Intervention: coupling MLP-linearization with epoch-level D2D resampling}
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:205:The CX-J1 intervention combines the MLP-only linearization protocol with the Ensemble HAT resampling protocol under global $NL=2.0$. During hardware-aware training, the MLP blocks receive $NL=1.0$ (ideal write), while all attention-side analog layers---QKV projection and attention output projection---experience the severe $NL=2.0$ surrogate. Crucially, the D2D mismatch field is no longer fixed: one fresh realization is drawn at the start of each training epoch and held constant across all mini-batches within that epoch, exactly as in canonical Ensemble HAT.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:211:The CX-J1 checkpoint achieves $91.36\,\%$ source-domain accuracy---the highest source-domain score of any severe-NL configuration tested, exceeding even the canonical $NL=1.0$ Ensemble HAT baseline of $91.13\,\%$. The joint training objective is therefore not merely compatible; it is \emph{synergistic} on the training-time array. The optimizer appears to exploit the distributional D2D exposure to find a more robust MLP-linearized solution than fixed-mask training permits.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:215:This result is methodologically decisive. The optimization-gap hypothesis predicted that adding Ensemble HAT resampling to MLP-linearization would lift fresh-instance accuracy toward the $86\,\%$ regime. Instead, the ceiling remains stuck near $30\,\%$. The distributional training objective that rescues D2D-mismatch collapse does \emph{not} rescue severe-NL collapse. The failure is therefore not a training artifact; it is a structural property of the first-order NL surrogate acting on the attention pathway.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:219:The immediate open question is \emph{why} the attention pathway under $NL=2.0$ enforces a $\sim$30\,\% ceiling regardless of the MLP configuration or the D2D training protocol. Three candidate mechanisms present themselves. First, the QKV projection may suffer from gradient-asymmetry-induced rank collapse: the LTP/LTD asymmetry of the $NL=2.0$ surrogate could bias the query and key matrices toward a low-rank subspace that is structurally incompatible with cross-instance D2D variation. Second, the attention output projection may experience catastrophic scale-recovery mismatch: the learned output-scale factors $s_{\ell}$ may calibrate to the particular interaction between D2D mismatch and NL-induced gradient distortion, producing a geometry that is stable on the training-time array but fragile on fresh instances. Third, the multi-head correlation structure may be disrupted: nonlinear write could decorrelate the heads in a way that is invisible to source-domain accuracy but fatal to generalization under mismatched spatial noise.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:250:The structural nature of the barrier is established by the joint-training result. If the $\sim$32\,\% ceiling were merely an optimization gap---a consequence of insufficient regularization or inadequate D2D diversity---then adding Ensemble HAT resampling should have broken it. Ensemble HAT raises fixed-mask HAT from $10.00\,\%$ to $86.37\pm1.54\,\%$ under identical mismatch statistics. Its failure to lift the MLP-linearized severe-NL checkpoint above $30.53\,\%$ demonstrates that the barrier is \emph{not} an optimization gap. No amount of D2D diversity can compensate for the structural incompatibility between the first-order NL surrogate and the attention pathway.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:254:The structural-limit hypothesis posits that the first-order nonlinear-write surrogate, when applied to the transformer attention pathway, imposes a \emph{fundamental generalization barrier} that is independent of the MLP configuration and independent of the D2D training protocol. The evidence for this hypothesis rests on three observations.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:258:Second, \textbf{global linearization is not the bottleneck}. The all-linear lane removes the NL surrogate from \emph{every} analog layer, reducing the problem to pure quantization, D2D mismatch, and C2C noise---the same non-idealities that canonical Ensemble HAT tolerates at $86.37\pm1.54\,\%$. Yet the all-linear checkpoint still caps at $32.60\pm9.18\,\%$. This suggests that the severe-NL regime fundamentally alters the optimization landscape in a way that persists even when nonlinearity is removed at train time, or that the fixed-mask training protocol independently enforces a similar ceiling. The CX-J1 result resolves this ambiguity.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:260:Third, \textbf{distributional training is not the bottleneck}. CX-J1 combines MLP linearization with the most effective distributional training protocol tested (Ensemble HAT) and still reaches only $30.53\pm7.07\,\%$. The attention pathway under $NL=2.0$ therefore enforces a generalization barrier that survives both MLP protection and D2D resampling. The barrier is structural because it is \emph{robust to independent perturbations of the training conditions}.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:262:The mechanistic candidate is \textbf{attention-pathway dominance}. Under the first-order $NL=2.0$ surrogate, the QKV and output-projection gradients acquire a state-dependent asymmetry that couples the learned spatial filters to the particular D2D pattern experienced during training. When the D2D pattern changes at deployment, the attention geometry---which is the core representational engine of the transformer---ceases to function. The MLP path, even when perfectly linearized, cannot compensate for a broken attention mechanism. This interpretation is consistent with the group-wise ablation of Chapter~\ref{chap:failure-mode-atlas}, which showed that attention-side linearization \emph{in isolation} collapses below the unmitigated baseline: the attention pathway is not merely sensitive to NL distortion; it is \emph{structurally coupled} to it.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:283:Ensemble HAT is defined by epoch-level resampling: one fresh D2D realization per epoch, held constant across all mini-batches within that epoch. But this cadence is a design choice, not a physical law. One could resample more frequently (per batch) or less frequently (every $k$ epochs). The problem is to determine which cadence optimizes the trade-off between distributional breadth and optimization stability.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:291:The intervention is a controlled ablation that trains three checkpoints under identical conditions except for the D2D resampling cadence. All checkpoints use the canonical Tiny-ViT V4 recipe: AdamW with learning rate $5\times10^{-4}$, weight decay $0.05$, cosine annealing over 50 epochs (the ablation used a shortened schedule for throughput), batch size 64, and 4-bit differential conductance mapping. The three conditions are:
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:293:    \item \textbf{Fixed-mask control:} One D2D realization drawn at initialization and frozen.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:294:    \item \textbf{Per-epoch (Ensemble HAT):} One fresh D2D realization at the start of each epoch.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:295:    \item \textbf{Per-batch control:} One fresh D2D realization for every forward pass during training.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:309:These numbers are not directly comparable to the paper-locked $86.37\pm1.54\,\%$ fresh-instance mean because they come from a shorter training run and a held-out validation split. Their structural message, however, is clear: epoch-level resampling outperforms \emph{both} fixed-mask and per-batch alternatives even under a weakened training budget. The per-batch condition, despite exposing the model to far more D2D diversity, underperforms the per-epoch condition by $2.25$~pp and even underperforms fixed-mask training by $1.02$~pp.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:316:    \caption{Fresh-instance robustness and D2D-resampling cadence ablation, reproduced from the manuscript supplementary package (Supplementary Figure~S4). \textbf{Left:} standard HAT collapses to chance on every fresh array, whereas Ensemble HAT preserves robust accuracy. \textbf{Right:} held-out accuracy under different resampling cadences in a 50-epoch scan. Epoch-level resampling reaches $88.41\,\%$, exceeding fixed-mask ($87.18\,\%$) and per-batch ($86.16\,\%$) controls.}
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:322:This interpretation is reinforced by the spatial-versus-i.i.d.~ablation discussed in the supplementary package. When the D2D field is replaced by independent per-layer i.i.d.~noise drawn every epoch, fresh-instance accuracy drops to approximately $75\,\%$; when it is drawn every batch, it drops further to approximately $70\,\%$. The structured spatial correlation of the true D2D field, combined with the block-stationary exposure schedule, is the load-bearing ingredient.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:357:The per-epoch condition outperforms fixed-mask by $1.23$~pp, a meaningful gain. But per-batch underperforms fixed-mask by $1.02$~pp, despite providing orders of magnitude more D2D diversity. This inversion is the signature of a plateau with negative returns beyond the optimum. The optimizer does not merely stop benefiting from additional frequency; it is actively harmed by it.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:361:The plateau is also visible in the spatial-versus-i.i.d.~ablation. When the structured D2D field is replaced by i.i.d.~per-layer noise, per-epoch resampling drops fresh-instance accuracy to approximately $75\,\%$, and per-batch drops it further to approximately $70\,\%$. The $5$~pp gap between per-epoch and per-batch persists even when spatial structure is removed, suggesting that the plateau is not an artifact of the particular correlation structure of the D2D field but a more general property of block-stationary optimization.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:369:Finally, the plateau concept extends beyond D2D resampling to other distributional training objectives. For example, if proportional-noise HAT (Chapter~\ref{chap:hat-taxonomy}, \S\ref{sec:noise-profile-targeting}) is combined with epoch-level resampling, does the same plateau structure apply to the \emph{amplitude} of the proportional noise? Or does the state-dependent noise law change the shape of the frequency--accuracy curve? These questions are relevant for designing adaptive HAT schedules that vary both noise profile and resampling cadence during training.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:386:Second, the case studies reinforce a general principle with an important boundary condition: \emph{the training objective matters more than the physical surrogate, but only when the surrogate does not impose a structural barrier}. Ensemble HAT recovers $86.37\pm1.54\,\%$ with the canonical $NL=1.0$ surrogate and uniform noise because the D2D-mismatch distortion is a \emph{random instance label} that distributional training can average away. The severe-NL surrogate, by contrast, is not merely a random label; it is a \emph{state-dependent gradient asymmetry} that reshapes the attention geometry in a way that is incompatible with cross-instance transfer. Distributional training cannot average away a structural deformation of the representational manifold.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:388:The all-linear severe-NL checkpoint, which removes the write-nonlinearity surrogate entirely, reaches only $32.60\pm9.18\,\%$ on fresh instances because it was trained with a fixed D2D mask. The physical simplification (ideal write) is outweighed by the training-objective deficiency (single-instance memorization). But the CX-J1 joint-training experiment shows that even when the training-objective deficiency is corrected (Ensemble HAT resampling), the severe-NL ceiling remains. The physical surrogate \emph{does} matter when it interacts structurally with the attention pathway.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:392:Third, the cadence ablation and frequency-plateau analysis show that block-stationary exposure is the load-bearing ingredient for D2D-mismatch robustness. Per-epoch resampling outperforms both fixed-mask and per-batch alternatives because it provides enough forward-path stability for the optimizer to descend under each instance, while the sequence of epochs provides enough distributional breadth to prevent instance-specific overfitting. The optimal cadence is not the maximum cadence; it is the cadence that balances stability and diversity. This principle holds for D2D mismatch but does not rescue severe-NL collapse.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:396:Fourth, the MLP-only, all-linear, and joint-training case studies expose an \emph{honesty gap} between source-domain rescue and deployment-grade transfer that is even starker than the D2D-mismatch case. A researcher who reports only the $91.36\,\%$ CX-J1 source-domain accuracy would conclude that severe NL is solved. The fresh-instance evaluation of $30.53\pm7.07\,\%$ shows that it is not. This gap has practical implications for experimental design and literature review: any analog-deployment paper that reports only ``noisy accuracy'' on the training-time instance may be overstating deployability by more than $60$~pp. The fresh-instance protocol ($10\times5$) should be treated as a minimal bar for claiming deployment-grade robustness.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:408:\textbf{Ensemble HAT} (\S\ref{sec:case-ensemble-hat}) addresses hardware-instance overfitting by replacing fixed-mask training with epoch-level D2D resampling. The intervention recovers $86.37\pm1.54\,\%$ fresh-instance accuracy, generalizes to the OPECT literature profile at $88.53\pm0.08\,\%$, and tolerates moderate spatially correlated D2D ($84.57\pm2.39\,\%$ at $\rho=0.3$). The open question is compound-stress robustness and the location of the optimal cadence knee.
./paper/thesis/chapter_5_mitigation.tex:416:\textbf{The $\sim$30\,\% ceiling} (\S\ref{sec:structural-limit}) argues that the convergence of three independent mitigations to the same fresh-instance regime is empirical evidence of a structural barrier. The first-order NL surrogate, when applied to the transformer attention pathway, imposes a fundamental generalization limit that survives MLP linearization, global linearization, and distributional D2D training. Falsification experiments (CX-J1b/c/d) are proposed.

./report_md/Gemini.md:131:- **Tiny-ViT V3**: Trained **WITH** device-to-device (D2D) noise active (result: 89.54%).
./report_md/Gemini.md:170:- **The Error**: The script is freshly resampling D2D offsets for "noisy" layers. This destroys the hardware-instance-specific adaptation learned during HAT training (V4).
./report_md/Gemini.md:172:  1. **Disable D2D Resampling**: Must use the native D2D buffers saved in the checkpoint.
./report_md/Gemini.md:184:1. Codex: Fix `run_layer_sensitivity.py` immediately (No D2D resampling).
./report_md/Gemini.md:223:- **Scale Masking & ADC Threshold**: The narrative correctly centers on our finding that 4-bit scale recovery effectively masks standard C2C/D2D noise, making the 6-bit ADC bottleneck the most critical architectural constraint.
./report_md/Gemini.md:244:- **Hardware-Instance Generalization Training**: To combat Transformer overfitting to a single hardware instance (Task 12 failure), we could introduce "Ensemble D2D Resampling" during HAT. By periodically resampling the D2D masks during training, the model would be forced to learn truly noise-invariant features rather than adapting to one specific chip realization.
./report_md/Gemini.md:253:- **V3 (D2D-only train)**: 44.06% (Massive -42.88 pp degradation)
./report_md/Gemini.md:259:- **V3 (D2D-only train)**: 4.81% (Train accuracy 99.9%, but completely fails to generalize. Pure hardware-instance overfitting).
./report_md/Gemini.md:453:- **Tiny-ViT V3**: Trained **WITH** device-to-device (D2D) noise active (result: 89.54%).
./report_md/Gemini.md:492:- **The Error**: The script is freshly resampling D2D offsets for "noisy" layers. This destroys the hardware-instance-specific adaptation learned during HAT training (V4).
./report_md/Gemini.md:494:  1. **Disable D2D Resampling**: Must use the native D2D buffers saved in the checkpoint.
./report_md/Gemini.md:506:1. Codex: Fix `run_layer_sensitivity.py` immediately (No D2D resampling).
./report_md/Gemini.md:545:- **Scale Masking & ADC Threshold**: The narrative correctly centers on our finding that 4-bit scale recovery effectively masks standard C2C/D2D noise, making the 6-bit ADC bottleneck the most critical architectural constraint.
./report_md/Gemini.md:566:- **Hardware-Instance Generalization Training**: To combat Transformer overfitting to a single hardware instance (Task 12 failure), we could introduce "Ensemble D2D Resampling" during HAT. By periodically resampling the D2D masks during training, the model would be forced to learn truly noise-invariant features rather than adapting to one specific chip realization.
./report_md/Gemini.md:575:- **V3 (D2D-only train)**: 44.06% (Massive -42.88 pp degradation)

./report_md/Gemini.md:659:3. **Expected Noise Degradation**: C3's test accuracy (23.86%) is evaluated **with noise** (D2D+C2C), whereas it was trained without noise. This massive gap is exactly the "noise degradation" we want to prove!

./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:19:The manuscript results are intentionally anchored to a first-order behavioral model: uniform Gaussian device-to-device (D2D) mismatch, scalar cycle-to-cycle (C2C) noise, a gradient-scaling surrogate for nonlinear write, double-exponential retention with fixed-temperature coefficients, and scalar placeholders for circuit-level parasitics.  Those simplifications were necessary to keep the narrative focused on the core algorithm--device boundary---the failure of fixed-mask hardware-aware training (HAT), the recovery via Ensemble HAT, and the severe-write-nonlinearity ceiling.  But the manuscript also candidly lists the deferred physics as limitations (manuscript limitations section): spatially structured mismatch beyond i.i.d., heavy-tailed conductance distributions, geometry-aware IR drop, sneak-path currents, and temperature-dependent drift.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:28:    \item \textbf{Correlated D2D} (Section~\ref{sec:correlated-d2d-full}): the manuscript reported a bounded-degradation sweep under separable AR(1) spatial correlation; here the full result is presented with statistical interpretation.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:39:\section{Correlated-D2D full result: spatial structure beyond the i.i.d.~assumption}
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:44:Every canonical result in the manuscript assumes i.i.d.~Gaussian D2D mismatch: each crossbar element samples its offset independently.  Real fabricated arrays, however, exhibit spatially correlated mismatch because of wafer-level process gradients, optical write non-uniformity across the programming aperture, and thermal coupling during conductance tuning (manuscript limitations section).  The manuscript therefore included a supplementary correlated-D2D ablation as a robustness probe, but it did not present the full statistical interpretation or connect the sweep to the failure-mode atlas developed in Chapter~\ref{chap:failure-mode-atlas}.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:55:where $h_{k}(\rho) = \sqrt{1-\rho^{2}}\, \rho^{|k|}$ is the normalized AR(1) impulse response and the boundary is handled by reflection padding.  The parameter $\rho \in [0,1)$ controls the spatial decay of correlation: $\rho=0$ recovers the i.i.d.~baseline, while larger $\rho$ produces smooth low-frequency gradients across the array.  The marginal variance is preserved at $\sigma^{2}_{\text{D2D}}$ by construction, so the only difference between correlation levels is the \emph{spatial structure}, not the amplitude.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:65:    \caption{Fresh-instance robustness of Ensemble HAT under spatially correlated D2D mismatch ($10$ fresh arrays $\times$ $5$ Monte Carlo evaluations).  All values are drawn from the same canonical checkpoint to isolate the effect of correlation structure.}
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:101:The canonical D2D model (Eq.~\ref{eq:d2d-model} in Chapter~\ref{chap:framework}) draws each element's mismatch from a zero-mean Gaussian with standard deviation $\sigma_{\text{D2D}} \Delta G$.  Gaussian noise is analytically convenient and often a good approximation for mid-range device counts, but it is not a physical law.  Organic thin-film transistors and memristive crossbars can exhibit \emph{heavy-tailed} conductance distributions because of filamentary variability, localized trap states, and spatial inhomogeneity in the active layer \citep{organicvariability2025}.  When the tail is heavy enough, rare outlier devices exert disproportionate influence on the analog matrix-vector product, potentially pushing the accuracy distribution into a qualitatively different regime than the one predicted by Gaussian statistics.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:107:A reference implementation stub exists at \texttt{scripts/\_gpt/eval\_heavy\_tailed\_d2d.py}.  The stub mirrors the existing fresh-instance evaluation harness (the same harness used for the correlated-D2D sweep of Section~\ref{sec:correlated-d2d-full}) but replaces the Gaussian sampler with a \texttt{sample\_heavy\_tailed\_d2d\_mask()} function that is currently a \texttt{NotImplementedError}.  The stub exposes the following command-line interface:
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:111:    \item \texttt{--tail-scale}: multiplicative severity factor $s_{\text{tail}} \in \{1.5, 2.0, 3.0\}$ relative to nominal $\sigma_{\text{D2D}}$.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:124:where $(\mu_{\ln}, \sigma_{\ln})$ are chosen so that the median of the tail component matches the nominal Gaussian scale, while the upper tail is inflated by the factor $s_{\text{tail}}$.  Truncation at $\epsilon_{\max} = 3\,\sigma_{\text{D2D}}\,s_{\text{tail}}$ prevents physically un-realizable conductance excursions (negative conductance or values beyond $G_{\max}$).  The log-normal family is attractive because it arises naturally from multiplicative process noise: if several independent fabrication steps each contribute a small multiplicative perturbation, their product is log-normally distributed.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:208:    \item Keep D2D and C2C active so the experiment measures interaction rather than a clean-room IR-only effect.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:237:    \sigma_{\text{D2D}}(T) = \sigma_{\text{D2D},0} \left[1 + \alpha_{\sigma}(T - T_{0})\right],
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:267:    \item \textbf{Ranking preservation:} the success criterion is not that accuracy stays at $86\,\%$ across the full range---that is unrealistic for an isothermally trained model---but that the \emph{relative risk ranking} observed in the manuscript persists.  Specifically, ADC resolution should remain the dominant accuracy gate, D2D mismatch should dominate in the operational envelope, and Ensemble HAT should still outperform fixed-mask HAT by a large margin at every temperature.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:274:Temperature effects do not operate in isolation.  The interconnect wire resistance $R_{w}$ (Section~\ref{sec:ir-drop}) has a positive temperature coefficient: $R_{w}(T) = R_{w,0}[1 + \text{PTC}\,(T-T_{0})]$.  As $T$ increases, $R_{w}$ increases, simultaneously worsening the spatial IR drop.  A fully coupled simulator would update $R_{w}(T)$ before computing the IR-drop attenuation map, then evaluate retention with temperature-accelerated time constants, and finally inject the temperature-inflated D2D field.  The modular profile interface makes this coupling straightforward in principle, but the joint parameter space ($T$, $R_{w}$, $\tau$, $\sigma_{\text{D2D}}$) is large enough that a systematic sweep would require $\sim$200 GPU-hours.  This section therefore limits itself to outlining the coupling; full execution is deferred.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:320:    \item \textbf{Inferential scale recalibration with reference cells:} instead of co-tracking the D2D buffers with the same decay law (which is analytically exact in simulation but physically approximate), periodically read out dedicated reference conductance pairs to update the digital gain coefficients.  The energy and latency cost of such recalibration is not included in the manuscript's $11.45\times$ energy-reduction estimate and should be treated as additional peripheral overhead.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:355:        Correlated D2D (\S\ref{sec:correlated-d2d-full}) & $10 \times 5$ AR(1) sweep at $\rho=0.0/0.3/0.5$; bounded degradation ($-1.76$~pp, $-4.20$~pp) locked. & Training \emph{with} correlated resampling (not evaluated). & Measured anisotropic covariance kernels from fabricated arrays. \\
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:368:\paragraph{Correlated D2D} is the only \textbf{core} extension in this chapter.  The empirical result---bounded degradation with preserved rank ordering---is locked and reproducible.  The open question is whether the same trend holds under measured spatial covariance rather than the AR(1) proxy.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:376:Every result in this thesis---core, extended, or future---is conditioned on the present first-order behavioral approximations.  The correlated-D2D sweep uses a separable AR(1) proxy, not a measured spatial kernel.  The heavy-tailed extension is defined but not executed.  The IR-drop model is a fast pre-pass, not a nodal solver.  The temperature framework uses literature Arrhenius coefficients, not measured activation energies.  The retention plateau is a double-exponential fit, not a physical drift law.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:378:This is not a weakness; it is a methodological choice.  The thesis treats each approximation as a \emph{decision aid} that ranks which physics extensions deserve experimental priority.  The correlated-D2D result tells a fabrication team that moderate spatial structure is survivable.  The IR-drop outline tells an array architect that tile geometry matters more than exact line resistance.  The temperature framework tells a packaging engineer that dynamic range erosion at the hot edge is the dominant risk.  These rankings are the thesis-level contribution of the physical-realism extensions, even when the full GPU results are deferred.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:384:    \item Hardware-instance overfitting (the $10.00\,\%$ collapse) is \emph{mitigated} by correlated D2D: the degradation is bounded rather than catastrophic.
./paper/thesis/chapter_6_physical_realism.tex:400:    \item \textbf{Correlated D2D} (Section~\ref{sec:correlated-d2d-full}): the full $10 \times 5$ AR(1) sweep is presented and interpreted.  Ensemble HAT fresh-instance accuracy degrades from $86.33\pm1.61\,\%$ (i.i.d.) to $84.57\pm2.39\,\%$ ($\rho=0.3$) to $82.12\pm3.95\,\%$ ($\rho=0.5$).  The degradation is bounded, rank order is preserved, and the worst-case instance remains far from chance level.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:22:    \item \textbf{Correlated-D2D bounded degradation}---the measurable but non-catastrophic loss of fresh-instance accuracy when spatially structured mismatch replaces the i.i.d.~assumption.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:35:Standard hardware-aware training fixes a single device-to-device (D2D) mismatch field $M_{0}$ for the entire training run, optimizing
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:44:The signature is a deterministic, near-chance collapse when the trained model is evaluated on a fresh hardware instance---a new fixed D2D realization $M' \sim p(M)$ drawn from the same distribution but never seen during training.  For Tiny-ViT V4 (the canonical uniform-noise HAT checkpoint), this collapse is exact: $10.00\pm0.00\,\%$ on CIFAR-10, i.e. the single-class baseline, across $10$ fresh arrays with $5$ Monte Carlo evaluations each (manuscript transferability section; Supplementary Note~S1).  A dedicated no-AMP control confirmed that the collapsed predictor is a deterministic training outcome, not a numerical artifact of mixed-precision autocast.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:150:Two features of the signature are diagnostically important.  First, the standard deviation across fresh instances is large ($7.72$~pp for MLP-only, $9.18$~pp for all-linear), indicating that some fresh arrays are tolerable while others are catastrophic.  This bimodal-like spread is qualitatively different from the tight $1.61$~pp spread of canonical Ensemble HAT and suggests that the MLP-linearized checkpoint has learned a fragile compensation that is sensitive to the particular D2D pattern it encounters.  Second, the all-linear upper bound (row~f) does not materially outperform MLP-only ($32.60$~pp vs.~$32.12$~pp), confirming that broader linearization does not convert the severe-NL ablation into a deployment-grade mitigation.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:158:The most consequential open question is whether a \emph{joint} training objective that couples severe-NL compensation with Ensemble HAT resampling can break the $32\,\%$ ceiling.  The current MLP-only and all-linear lanes were trained with a fixed D2D mask; it is possible that epoch-level D2D resampling would regularize the NL-compensated checkpoint sufficiently to recover deployment-grade transfer.  This ``joint MLP-linear + Ensemble HAT'' experiment is flagged in the thesis outline as a high-priority next step (Chapter Outline, \S6).  A second open question is whether the large cross-instance variance reflects an instability that could be damped by larger mini-batches, longer training, or explicit variance-regularization penalties.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:162:\section{Failure mode: Correlated-D2D bounded degradation}
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:167:All canonical results in the manuscript assume i.i.d.~Gaussian D2D mismatch: each crossbar element samples its offset independently.  Real fabricated arrays, however, exhibit spatially \emph{correlated} mismatch because of wafer-level process gradients, optical write non-uniformity across the aperture, and thermal coupling during programming (manuscript limitations section).  The trigger of this failure mode is therefore not a change in mismatch \emph{amplitude} but a change in its \emph{spatial structure}: replacing i.i.d.~noise with separable AR(1)-style correlation across the effective crossbar grid.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:177:Rank ordering is preserved across all tested levels, with no instance collapsing below $73.7\,\%$ (Supplementary Note~S2).  The dashed horizontal line at $10.00\,\%$ marks the standard-HAT collapse baseline, emphasizing that correlated D2D produces \emph{bounded} degradation rather than a new failure basin.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:182:    \caption{Fresh-instance robustness under spatially correlated D2D mismatch (reused from manuscript supplementary package, Fig.~S5).  Error bars show cross-instance standard deviation over $10$ fresh arrays, with $5$ Monte Carlo evaluations per array, for the canonical Tiny-ViT V4 Ensemble HAT checkpoint.}
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:194:The correlated-D2D sweep uses a separable AR(1) model, which is a convenient mathematical proxy rather than a measured spatial covariance kernel.  Real organic optoelectronic arrays may exhibit anisotropic correlation (stronger along wordlines than bitlines due to shared gate buses), heavy-tailed conductance distributions, or long-range wafer-scale gradients that are not captured by a first-order separable model.  Whether Ensemble HAT (or a correlated variant) remains robust under these richer spatial structures is an open empirical question that can only be answered once fabricated-array characterization data become available.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:207:The signature is a \emph{two-phase decay curve} with a rapid early drop followed by a broad asymptotic plateau.  Figure~\ref{fig:retention-curve-repeat} (reused from manuscript Fig.~7 / Supplementary Fig.~S6) shows the corrected V8 retention trajectory for Tiny-ViT under dynamic scale recalibration and co-decay of the retained D2D buffers:
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:227:The primary mitigation is \emph{dynamic scale recalibration} at inference time: periodically recomputing the layer-wise scale factors $s_{\ell}$ from the present (drifted) effective conductances.  Under the present first-order model, this recalibration is analytically exact because the D2D buffers and cycle-to-cycle (C2C) noise are co-tracked with the same decay law.  In a physical system, the equivalent operation would require intermittent readout of reference cells or dummy conductance pairs to update the digital gain coefficients.  The energy and latency cost of such recalibration is not included in the manuscript's $11.45\times$ energy-reduction estimate (manuscript energy-results section) and should be treated as an additional peripheral overhead.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:233:The $79\,\%$ plateau is a consequence of the specific $\tau_{1}$, $\tau_{2}$, and $A_{0}$ values extracted from one literature source.  Real organic devices may exhibit faster slow modes, temperature-accelerated drift, or state-dependent decay that invalidates the uniform model.  Whether the plateau shifts upward (better materials) or downward (worse environmental conditions) determines whether retention is a solved peripheral problem or a fundamental deployment blocker.  A second open question is the interaction between retention drift and the other failure modes in this atlas: does a retained D2D field that has drifted for $1000$~s become more or less susceptible to hardware-instance overfitting when transferred to a fresh array?  The present framework evaluates each non-ideality in isolation; their joint statistics remain unexplored.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:244:The most important cross-cutting distinction is between \emph{source-domain rescue} and \emph{deployment-grade transfer}.  Standard HAT rescues source-domain accuracy on the training-time array; the MLP-only linearization rescues source-domain accuracy under severe NL; even the unmitigated $NL=2.0$ baseline reaches $27.72\,\%$, which is above chance.  But none of these rescues survive fresh-instance evaluation.  Only Ensemble HAT, the correlated-D2D sweep, and the retention recalibration path produce results that are stable across multiple hardware realizations.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:250:A second theme is the non-uniform sensitivity of the transformer anatomy to different physics extensions.  Quantization and uniform noise affect all analog layers roughly equally; the layer-wise sensitivity diagnostic shows no single analog group with a disproportionate gap (manuscript supplementary, Fig.~S1).  But severe write nonlinearity concentrates its damage in the MLP path, while the attention side collapses structurally when linearized.  Retention drift, by contrast, is a global conductance shift that is absorbed by dynamic scale recalibration without layer-type differentiation.  Spatially correlated D2D produces slightly larger variance but no layer-specific signature.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:256:Every entry in the atlas is conditioned on the present first-order behavioral approximations.  The $NL=2.0$ surrogate is a gradient-scaling approximation, not a pulse-faithful model; the retention decay is a double-exponential fit, not a physical drift law; the correlated D2D uses a separable AR(1) proxy, not a measured spatial covariance kernel.  The manuscript candidly lists these as limitations (manuscript limitations section), but the atlas structure makes their role explicit: \emph{each failure mode is a boundary of the current approximation, not necessarily a boundary of the physical device.}
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:262:All five failure modes were evaluated in isolation.  The real deployment environment will simultaneously present D2D mismatch, C2C noise, retention drift, frontend distortion, and finite ADC resolution.  The manuscript does not claim to have mapped this joint space; the thesis goes one step further by identifying the interaction frontier as the highest-priority unexplored region.  In particular:
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:265:    \item Does the $32\,\%$ MLP-only fresh-instance ceiling rise or fall when correlated D2D is added to the severe-NL regime?
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:279:    \item \textbf{Hardware-instance overfitting} (\S\ref{sec:failure-instance-overfitting}): Triggered by fixing one D2D mask for the entire training run; signature is a deterministic $10.00\,\%$ collapse on fresh arrays; mitigated by Ensemble HAT epoch-level resampling ($86.37\pm1.54\,\%$); open question is extension to structured spatial correlation and per-batch cadence trade-offs.
./paper/thesis/chapter_4_failure_modes.tex:282:    \item \textbf{Correlated-D2D bounded degradation} (\S\ref{sec:failure-correlated-d2d}): Triggered by spatially structured mismatch ($\rho=0.3$, $0.5$); signature is monotonic mean degradation ($84.57\,\%$, $82.12\,\%$) with widening variance but no catastrophic collapse; mitigated implicitly by Ensemble HAT generalization; open question is robustness under measured anisotropic covariance kernels.

./report_md/A1_implementation_plan.md:100:NL_LTP=+1.0, NL_LTD=-1.0
./report_md/A1_implementation_plan.md:112:**__init__**: 同 nn.Linear 参数 + config。`register_buffer('d2d_noise', randn * sigma_d2d * G_range)` 固定 D2D 噪声。
./report_md/A1_implementation_plan.md:121:- 训练 (HAT)：量化+噪声全开，STE 传梯度，D2D 固定，C2C 每步重采样，retention OFF
./report_md/A1_implementation_plan.md:128:- D2D 噪声的 scale = sigma_d2d × G_range（相对于电导范围的比例噪声）
./report_md/A1_implementation_plan.md:159:- 验证 D2D 固定：两次 forward，d2d_noise 不变
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:35:The ceiling is almost certainly an artifact of \emph{fixed-mask} training.  The MLP-linear and all-linear lanes were trained with a single D2D realization frozen for the entire run.  The optimizer therefore learned a source-domain compensation that is tightly coupled to the spatial signature of that one instance.  When the instance changes, the compensation becomes invalid, producing the large cross-instance variance observed in Table~\ref{tab:fresh-instance-nl} of Chapter~\ref{chap:failure-mode-atlas}.  The obvious question---and the central thesis-completion experiment---is whether \emph{epoch-level D2D resampling} (Ensemble HAT) can regularize the severe-NL compensation sufficiently to restore deployment-grade transfer.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:37:The experimental design is as follows.  Starting from the canonical Ensemble HAT checkpoint ($86.37\pm1.54\,\%$ fresh-instance mean), we apply a weights-only warm-start that resets optimizer state, scheduler state, and epoch counters, ensuring that the short NL-compensation run cannot skip training because of stale metadata.  During the subsequent fine-tuning phase, the MLP blocks retain their linearized $\mathrm{NL}=1.0$ surrogate while the attention blocks are exposed to the full $\mathrm{NL}=2.0$ distortion, and---crucially---the D2D mismatch field is resampled at the start of every epoch.  The primary target metric is fresh-instance accuracy under the $10 \times 5$ protocol.  A target of $\ge 80\,\%$ would validate that the two largest failure modes---instance overfitting and severe write nonlinearity---are \emph{jointly} mitigable.  Anything below $50\,\%$ would confirm a fundamental incompatibility between first-order NL surrogates and distributional training.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:53:All empirical anchors in this thesis---the $86.37\pm1.54\,\%$ Ensemble HAT recovery, the $27.72\pm0.82\,\%$ severe-NL collapse, the correlated-D2D bounded degradation ($86.33\pm1.61\,\%$ at $\rho=0$, $84.57\pm2.39\,\%$ at $\rho=0.3$, falling to $82.12\pm3.95\,\%$ at $\rho=0.5$), and the $79\,\%$ retention plateau---were measured on CIFAR-10 with Tiny-ViT.  That choice was deliberate: it isolated the physics--algorithm interaction from the confounding effects of dataset scale and resolution.  But it leaves a critical question unanswered.  Are the ranking logic and the failure-mode boundaries a small-dataset curiosity, or do they survive the transition to larger vision tasks?
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:55:The recommended pilot, specified under the G-AA2 protocol, uses ImageNet-100---a curated 100-class subset of ImageNet-1k---rather than the full 1k dataset.  The model remains the Tiny-ViT family, preserving the mixed-signal mapping logic (analog linear layers, digital control-heavy operators) that has been the constant across all preceding chapters.  The training protocol begins with the canonical Ensemble HAT cadence: AdamW, cosine annealing, per-epoch D2D resampling, 4-bit differential conductance mapping.  The only manipulated variable is dataset scale.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:59:The success criterion is qualitative, not quantitative: the pilot succeeds if the \emph{ranking} of mitigation strategies survives.  Ensemble HAT should still outperform fixed-mask HAT; the severe-NL MLP-linear rescue should still underperform on fresh instances; correlated D2D should still produce bounded rather than catastrophic degradation.  If these rankings hold, the deployment envelope derived in this thesis is a \emph{scalable} design principle.  If they collapse, the envelope is regime-specific and requires re-derivation at every scale.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:73:Tiny-ViT has approximately five million parameters.  A modest modern LLM has seven to seventy \emph{billion}.  Mapping such a model to analog crossbars requires tiling across hundreds or thousands of arrays, each with its own D2D mismatch field, scale-recovery factor, and ADC periphery.  The dimensionality of the deployment distribution therefore explodes: instead of resampling a single global mismatch map, HAT would need to resample a \emph{collection} of tile-level maps, possibly with across-tile process gradients that introduce long-range spatial correlation.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:91:Multi-head attention offers natural parallelism: each head can be mapped to a separate crossbar array, allowing simultaneous computation of multiple attention subspaces.  In the vision experiments, the number of heads is small (e.g., four or eight) and the mismatch per head is treated as part of the global D2D field.  In LLMs, the number of heads is larger (thirty-two to ninety-six in current architectures), and the mismatch per head may introduce \emph{structural} variation in the multi-head ensemble.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:93:If one head suffers severe D2D mismatch while its neighbours are clean, the learned aggregation of head outputs may amplify the erroneous head.  Ensemble HAT training would need to resample mismatch not just per epoch but per \emph{head group}, ensuring that the model learns robust aggregation across head-level noise realizations.  The optimal granularity---per head, per head-group, or per layer---is unknown and likely task-dependent.  This is a direct analogue of the cadence question studied in Chapter~\ref{chap:hat-taxonomy}: just as per-epoch resampling outperforms per-batch for the global mismatch field, there may be an optimal head-resampling cadence that balances diversity and optimization stability.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:98:Despite these differences, the \emph{core principle} of this thesis remains valid for language models: train over the deployment distribution of hardware instances, not a single instance.  The practical challenge is cost.  Training an LLM is already computationally expensive; adding D2D resampling at tile or head granularity multiplies the forward-pass overhead.  Several efficiency strategies suggest themselves.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:100:First, resample mismatch only for the analog-mapped layers, keeping the digital embedding, normalization, and softmax layers deterministic.  This reduces the noise dimensionality without sacrificing the key physics.  Second, use a \emph{lightweight} surrogate for the mismatch field: instead of drawing full spatial maps, sample low-rank approximations that capture the dominant process gradients.  Third, evaluate generalization not by classification accuracy but by \emph{perplexity} on a held-out token sequence, measuring the fresh-instance protocol as a perplexity gap between training-time and unseen tile configurations.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:114:The current framework uses first-order behavioural surrogates.  Write nonlinearity is approximated by scaling the STE gradient (Equations~(\ref{eq:nl-ltp})--(\ref{eq:nl-ltd}) of Chapter~\ref{chap:framework}); retention is modelled by a double-exponential fit with literature-derived time constants; correlated D2D uses a separable AR(1) proxy.  These approximations were necessary to make the design-space sweeps tractable, but they create an \emph{approximation ceiling}: every failure mode in Chapter~\ref{chap:failure-mode-atlas} is a boundary of the surrogate, not necessarily of the physical device.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:122:The blocker is not measurement technique---the required instruments are standard---but \emph{statistical representativeness}.  Organic devices often suffer from batch-to-batch variability that exceeds within-batch D2D spread.  A profile derived from one fabrication run may not generalise to the next, undermining the very notion of a canonical ``device profile.''  Solving this requires either run-to-run calibration protocols or Bayesian profile updating, neither of which is mature.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:127:Before committing to a full custom chip, the analog forward path can be emulated on FPGA to validate digital control logic and evaluate end-to-end inference latency.  The FPGA would implement the complete mixed-signal pipeline: conductance quantization, D2D and cycle-to-cycle (C2C) noise injection, retention drift, inverse-gamma front-end compensation, ADC quantization with DNL, and dynamic scale recovery.  The weights are HAT-trained from the simulator; the FPGA merely executes the noisy forward pass in real time.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:169:This objective is structurally identical to adding a data-dependent noise regulariser to the loss landscape.  Drawing a fresh $M$ at each epoch convolves the empirical risk with the mismatch distribution, smoothing sharp minima and favouring flat regions that are compatible with many hardware instances.  The connection to randomized smoothing and implicit regularisation is immediate: the D2D resampling acts as a controlled perturbation of the forward path, and the straight-through estimator propagates the smoothed gradient back to the weights.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:216:Several insights follow from Equation~(\ref{eq:pac-bayes}).  First, the bound tightens as $O(1/\sqrt{E})$, explaining why longer training (more independent mismatch draws) improves fresh-instance accuracy up to a plateau.  Second, the KL term penalises overly complex posteriors; this provides a theoretical justification for weight decay and early stopping, both of which shrink the effective posterior volume.  Third, the bound is \emph{distribution-dependent}: if the training mismatch distribution $p_{\mathrm{train}}(M)$ differs from the deployment distribution $p_{\mathrm{deploy}}(M)$---for example, because of correlated D2D or batch-to-batch fabrication drift---the KL term must be replaced by a divergence between the two distributions, and the bound widens proportionally.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:233:With the instrument in hand, Chapter~\ref{chap:hat-instance-overfitting} asked the obvious question: if we train a vision transformer on a noisy analog array, does it survive deployment?  The answer was a stark $10.00\pm0.00\,\%$ collapse.  Standard hardware-aware training, which fixes a single device-to-device mismatch field for the entire training run, overfits that instance as if it were a hidden domain label.  The discovery is not that analog noise hurts accuracy---that was known---but that \emph{robustness to analog noise is not the same as robustness to hardware-instance shift}.  The remedy, Ensemble HAT, is simple in retrospect: resample the mismatch field at epoch boundaries so that the optimizer learns over a \emph{distribution} of arrays rather than memorising one sample from it.  The result---$86.37\pm1.54\,\%$ fresh-instance accuracy---is the empirical anchor of the thesis.
./paper/thesis/chapter_8_outlook.tex:239:If the first act built the instrument and discovered the central phenomenon, the second act mapped its boundaries.  Chapter~\ref{chap:failure-mode-atlas} organised the empirical landscape as a structured catalogue of named, reproducible breakdown patterns.  Each entry---hardware-instance overfitting, dual-attention nonlinear-write collapse, MLP-only diagnostic bound, correlated-D2D bounded degradation, retention plateau at $79\,\%$---is annotated with a trigger, a signature, a mitigation, and an open question.

./generate_synthetic_device_profiles_gpt.py:84:            NL_LTD=base.NL_LTD,
