    51	) -> dict:
    52	    print(f"\nEvaluating fresh-instance transfer for: {checkpoint_path}", flush=True)
    53
    54	    # Load groupwise setter
    55	    selector = build_selector(protected_group)
    56	    base.set_noise_for_eval = make_groupwise_setter(
    57	        selector=selector,
    58	        protected_nl_ltp=protected_nl_ltp,
    59	        protected_nl_ltd=protected_nl_ltd,
    60	        train_mode=False,
    61	        use_second_order_ste=use_second_order_ste,
    62	        delta_g_eff=delta_g_eff,
    63	        second_order_alpha=second_order_alpha,
    64	    )
    65
    66	    ckpt = torch.load(checkpoint_path, map_location=device, weights_only=False)
    67	    exp_cfg_dict = ckpt.get("exp_cfg", {})
    68	    valid_keys = {f.name for f in __import__("dataclasses").fields(TinyViTExperimentConfig)}
    69	    filtered = {k: v for k, v in exp_cfg_dict.items() if k in valid_keys}
    70	    cfg = TinyViTExperimentConfig(**filtered)
    71	    resolved_dataset = dataset or ckpt.get("dataset") or "cifar10"
    72	    head_weight = (ckpt.get("model_state_dict") or {}).get("head.fc.weight")
    73	    resolved_num_classes = num_classes
    74	    if resolved_num_classes is None:
    75	        if ckpt.get("num_classes") is not None:
    76	            resolved_num_classes = int(ckpt["num_classes"])
    77	        elif head_weight is not None:
    78	            resolved_num_classes = int(head_weight.shape[0])
    79	        else:
    80	            resolved_num_classes = 10
    81	    resolved_batch_size = eval_batch_size or 256
    82
    83	    _, testloader = get_dataloaders(
    84	        dataset=resolved_dataset,
    85	        batch_size=resolved_batch_size,
    86	        data_root=data_root,
    87	        num_workers=num_workers,
    88	    )
    89	    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
    90
    91	    instance_means = []
    92	    instance_rows = []
    93
    94	    for instance_idx in range(fresh_instances):
    95	        seed = 42 + instance_idx * 100
    96	        set_seed(seed)
    97	        model = build_model(cfg, num_classes=resolved_num_classes, device=device, pretrained=False)
    98	        model.load_state_dict(ckpt["model_state_dict"], strict=True)
    99	        resampled = resample_all_d2d_noise(model)
   100	        base.set_noise_for_eval(model, cfg)
   101
   102	        losses = []
   103	        accs = []
   104	        for _ in range(eval_runs):
   105	            loss, acc = evaluate(model, testloader, criterion, device, cfg, amp_enabled=False)
   106	            losses.append(float(loss))
   107	            accs.append(float(acc))
   108
   109	        mean_acc = mean(accs)
   110	        print(
   111	            f"  instance {instance_idx + 1:02d}/{fresh_instances}: "
   112	            f"mean_acc={mean_acc:.2f}% eval_runs={eval_runs}",
   113	            flush=True,
   114	        )
   115	        instance_means.append(mean_acc)
   116	        instance_rows.append(
   117	            {
   118	                "instance_index": instance_idx,
   119	                "seed": seed,
   120	                "resampled_modules": resampled,
   121	                "eval_runs": eval_runs,
   122	                "test_loss_mean": mean(losses),
   123	                "test_acc_mean": mean_acc,
   124	                "test_acc_std": stdev(accs) if len(accs) > 1 else 0.0,
   125	                "test_acc_raw": accs,
   126	            }
   127	        )
   128
   129	    result = {
   130	        "checkpoint_path": checkpoint_path,
   131	        "train_best_acc": float(ckpt.get("best_acc", float("nan"))),
   132	        "train_best_epoch": int(ckpt.get("best_epoch", ckpt.get("epoch", -1))),
   133	        "fresh_instances": fresh_instances,
   134	        "mc_runs_per_instance": eval_runs,
   135	        "dataset": resolved_dataset,
   136	        "num_classes": resolved_num_classes,
   137	        "eval_batch_size": resolved_batch_size,
   138	        "cross_instance_mean": mean(instance_means),
   139	        "cross_instance_std": stdev(instance_means) if len(instance_means) > 1 else 0.0,
   140	        "instance_means": instance_means,
   141	        "instances": instance_rows,
   142	    }
   143	    print(
   144	        f"Completed: {result['cross_instance_mean']:.2f}% +/- {result['cross_instance_std']:.2f}% "
   145	        f"across {fresh_instances} fresh instances",
   146	        flush=True,
   147	    )
   148	    return result
   149
   150
   151	def main():
   152	    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
   153	    parser.add_argument("--checkpoint", required=True)
   154	    parser.add_argument("--device", default="cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
   155	    parser.add_argument("--fresh-instances", type=int, default=10)
   156	    parser.add_argument("--eval-runs", type=int, default=5)
   157	    parser.add_argument("--data-root", default="./data")
   158	    parser.add_argument("--num-workers", type=int, default=0)
   159	    parser.add_argument("--dataset", default=None, help="Override dataset name; default: read from checkpoint")
   160	    parser.add_argument("--num-classes", type=int, default=None, help="Override class count; default: read from checkpoint/head shape")
   161	    parser.add_argument("--eval-batch-size", type=int, default=None, help="Override eval batch size; default: 256")
   162	    parser.add_argument("--protected-group", default="mlp")
   163	    parser.add_argument("--protected-nl-ltp", type=float, default=1.0)
   164	    parser.add_argument("--protected-nl-ltd", type=float, default=-1.0)
   165	    parser.add_argument("--use-second-order-ste", action="store_true")
   166	    parser.add_argument("--delta-g-eff", type=float, default=-1.0,
   167	                        help="Effective perturbation scale for curvature correction (negative=auto, 0=literal zero)")
   168	    parser.add_argument("--second-order-alpha", type=float, default=1.0)
   169	    parser.add_argument("--json-out", default="report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/joint_fresh_eval.json")
   170	    args = parser.parse_args()
   171
   172	    result = evaluate_fresh_instances_groupwise(
   173	        checkpoint_path=args.checkpoint,
   174	        device=args.device,
   175	        fresh_instances=args.fresh_instances,
   176	        eval_runs=args.eval_runs,
   177	        data_root=args.data_root,
   178	        num_workers=args.num_workers,
   179	        dataset=args.dataset,
   180	        eval_batch_size=args.eval_batch_size,
   181	        num_classes=args.num_classes,
   182	        protected_group=args.protected_group,
   183	        protected_nl_ltp=args.protected_nl_ltp,
   184	        protected_nl_ltd=args.protected_nl_ltd,
   185	        use_second_order_ste=args.use_second_order_ste,
   186	        delta_g_eff=args.delta_g_eff,
   187	        second_order_alpha=args.second_order_alpha,
   188	    )
   189
   190	    Path(args.json_out).parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
   191	    with open(args.json_out, "w") as f:
   192	        json.dump(result, f, indent=2)
   193	    print(f"JSON saved to: {args.json_out}")
   194
   195
   196	if __name__ == "__main__":
   197	    main()

   142	class RunLogger:
   143	    """Simple stdout + file logger for dry-run outputs."""
   144
   145	    def __init__(self, path: Optional[str] = None):
   146	        self.path = path
   147	        self._fh = None
   148	        if path:
   149	            ensure_parent_dir(path)
   150	            self._fh = open(path, "w", encoding="utf-8")
   151
   152	    def log(self, message: str = ""):
   153	        if message:
   154	            stamped = f"[{datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')}] {message}"
   155	        else:
   156	            stamped = ""
   157	        print(stamped)
   158	        if self._fh is not None:
   159	            self._fh.write(stamped + "\n")
   160	            self._fh.flush()
   161
   162	    def close(self):
   163	        if self._fh is not None:
   164	            self._fh.close()
   165	            self._fh = None
   166
   167
   168	def ensure_parent_dir(path: Optional[str]):
   169	    if not path:
   170	        return
   171	    parent = os.path.dirname(path)
   172	    if parent:
   173	        os.makedirs(parent, exist_ok=True)
   174
   175
   176	class TinyViTPhysicalFrontEnd(nn.Module):
   177	    """Optional physical front-end used by V6."""
   178
   179	    def __init__(self, dataset: str, gamma_phys: float = 1.0,
   180	                 alpha: float = 1.0, I_dark: float = 1e-10, shot_noise: bool = True):
   181	        super().__init__()
   182	        stats = DATASET_STATS[dataset]
   183	        self.inverse_gamma = InverseGammaPreprocessor(gamma_phys=gamma_phys, alpha=alpha)
   184	        self.photo_sim = PhotocurrentSimulator(
   185	            alpha=alpha,
   186	            I_dark=I_dark,
   187	            gamma_phys=gamma_phys,
   188	            shot_noise=shot_noise,
   189	        )
   190	        self.register_buffer("mean", torch.tensor(stats["mean"]).view(1, 3, 1, 1))
   191	        self.register_buffer("std", torch.tensor(stats["std"]).view(1, 3, 1, 1))
   192
   193	    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor, mode: str = "compensated") -> torch.Tensor:
   194	        if mode == "compensated":
   195	            p_in, _ = self.inverse_gamma(x)
   196	            i_out = self.photo_sim(p_in, mode="compensated")
   197	        elif mode == "raw":
   198	            i_out = self.photo_sim(x, mode="raw")
   199	        else:
   200	            raise ValueError(f"Unknown front-end mode: {mode}")
   201
   202	        batch = i_out.shape[0]
   203	        flat = i_out.view(batch, -1)
   204	        i_min = flat.min(dim=1, keepdim=True).values.view(batch, 1, 1, 1)
   205	        i_max = flat.max(dim=1, keepdim=True).values.view(batch, 1, 1, 1)
   206	        i_range = (i_max - i_min).clamp(min=1e-8)
   207	        x_norm = (i_out - i_min) / i_range
   208	        return (x_norm - self.mean.to(x.device)) / self.std.to(x.device)
   209
   210
   211	def get_num_classes(dataset: str, num_classes: Optional[int] = None) -> int:
   212	    """Resolve the number of output classes for a dataset."""
   213	    if num_classes is not None:
   214	        return num_classes
   215	    return DATASET_STATS[dataset]["num_classes"]
   216
   217
   218	def create_tinyvit_model(num_classes: int, pretrained: bool = False):
   219	    """Create a raw Tiny-ViT model via timm."""
   220	    return timm.create_model(MODEL_NAME, pretrained=pretrained, num_classes=num_classes)
   221
   222
   223	def resolve_experiment_ids(default_experiment: str,
   224	                           requested_experiments: Optional[Sequence[str]],
   225	                           configs: Dict[str, TinyViTExperimentConfig]) -> List[str]:
   226	    """Parse a comma-separated experiment list and validate against known configs."""
   227	    raw_ids = list(requested_experiments) if requested_experiments else [default_experiment]
   228	    tokens: List[str] = []
   229
   230	    for raw in raw_ids:
   231	        for token in raw.split(","):
   232	            token = token.strip()
   233	            if token:
   234	                tokens.append(token)
   235
   236	    if any(token.upper() == "ALL" for token in tokens):
   237	        return list(configs.keys())
   238
   239	    resolved: List[str] = []
   240	    seen = set()
   241	    for token in tokens:
   242	        if token not in configs:
   243	            raise ValueError(f"Unknown experiment '{token}'. Expected one of: {sorted(configs)}")
   244	        if token not in seen:
   245	            resolved.append(token)
   246	            seen.add(token)
   247
   248	    return resolved
   249
   250
   251	def build_model(exp_cfg: TinyViTExperimentConfig, num_classes: int, device: str,
   252	                pretrained: bool = False) -> nn.Module:
   253	    """Instantiate a Tiny-ViT and optionally convert it to hybrid analog/digital."""
   254	    model = create_tinyvit_model(num_classes=num_classes, pretrained=pretrained)
   255
   256	    if exp_cfg.use_hybrid:
   257	        analog_cfg = AnalogLinearConfig(
   258	            n_states=exp_cfg.n_states,
   259	            NL_LTP=exp_cfg.nl_ltp,
   260	            NL_LTD=exp_cfg.nl_ltd,
   261	            sigma_c2c=exp_cfg.sigma_c2c if exp_cfg.hat_training else 0.0,
   262	            sigma_d2d=exp_cfg.sigma_d2d,
   263	            noise_mode=exp_cfg.noise_mode,
   264	            noise_enabled=exp_cfg.hat_training and exp_cfg.noise_enabled,
   265	            restore_weight_scale=True,
   266	        )
   267	        model = convert_to_hybrid(model, config=analog_cfg, verbose=False)
   268	        set_retention(
   269	            model,
   270	            exp_cfg.inference_time if exp_cfg.retention_enabled else 0.0,
   271	            recalibrate_scale=exp_cfg.retention_enabled,
   272	            scale_d2d=exp_cfg.retention_enabled,
   273	        )
   274
   275	    return model.to(device)
   276
   277
   278	def set_noise_for_eval(model: nn.Module, exp_cfg: TinyViTExperimentConfig):
   279	    """Push evaluation noise parameters from *exp_cfg* into analog layers."""
   280	    for module in model.modules():
   281	        if isinstance(module, (AnalogLinear, AnalogConv2d)):
   282	            module.config.noise_enabled = exp_cfg.noise_enabled
   283	            module.config.sigma_c2c = exp_cfg.sigma_c2c
   284	            module.config.sigma_d2d = exp_cfg.sigma_d2d
   285	            module.config.noise_mode = exp_cfg.noise_mode
   286	            module.config.NL_LTP = exp_cfg.nl_ltp
   287	            module.config.NL_LTD = exp_cfg.nl_ltd
   288
   289
   290	def set_noise_for_train(model: nn.Module, exp_cfg: TinyViTExperimentConfig):
   291	    """Push training noise parameters from *exp_cfg* into analog layers."""
   292	    for module in model.modules():
   293	        if isinstance(module, (AnalogLinear, AnalogConv2d)):
   294	            if exp_cfg.hat_training:
   295	                module.config.noise_enabled = exp_cfg.noise_enabled
   296	                module.config.sigma_c2c = exp_cfg.sigma_c2c
   297	            elif exp_cfg.noise_enabled:
   298	                # Standard noisy training keeps fixed D2D mismatch active but
   299	                # disables per-forward C2C resampling. This avoids an overly
   300	                # optimistic train/eval gap for Tiny-ViT while preserving the
   301	                # HAT vs. non-HAT distinction.
   302	                module.config.noise_enabled = True
   303	                module.config.sigma_c2c = 0.0
   304	            else:
   305	                module.config.noise_enabled = False
   306	                module.config.sigma_c2c = 0.0
   307	            module.config.sigma_d2d = exp_cfg.sigma_d2d
   308	            module.config.noise_mode = exp_cfg.noise_mode
   309	            module.config.NL_LTP = exp_cfg.nl_ltp
   310	            module.config.NL_LTD = exp_cfg.nl_ltd
   311
   312
   313	def set_retention(model: nn.Module, inference_time: float,
   314	                  recalibrate_scale: bool = False,
   315	                  scale_d2d: bool = False):
   316	    """Configure retention decay on all analog layers in *model*."""
   317	    for module in model.modules():
   318	        if isinstance(module, (AnalogLinear, AnalogConv2d)):
   319	            module.config.retention_enabled = inference_time > 0
   320	            module.config.inference_time = inference_time

codex
The JSON itself records `nl_ltp=2.0` and `nl_ltd=-2.0`, and `run_postfix_eval.sh` passes those flags. The script invoked there is `eval_fresh_instances_postfix.py`, so I’m inspecting that exact evaluator rather than relying on the older joint evaluator.

 succeeded in 0ms:
Cannot open netlink socket: Operation not permitted
     1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2	"""Fresh-instance evaluator for post-fix rerun checkpoints."""
     3	import argparse
     4	import json
     5	import torch
     6	import numpy as np
     7	from pathlib import Path
     8
     9	from inference_analysis_utils import load_model_bundle, run_mc_eval, set_uniform_noise
    10	from train_tinyvit import get_v_experiment_configs
    11
    12	def evaluate_on_fresh_instances(model_type, exp_id, checkpoint_path, device,
    13	                                num_instances=10, mc_runs_per_instance=5,
    14	                                nl_ltp=None, nl_ltd=None, noise_mode=None):
    15	    bundle = load_model_bundle(model_type, exp_id, device, checkpoint_path)
    16	    cfg = bundle.exp_cfg
    17
    18	    # Override NL if specified (for post-fix evaluation of checkpoints trained with specific NL)
    19	    if nl_ltp is not None:
    20	        cfg.nl_ltp = nl_ltp
    21	    if nl_ltd is not None:
    22	        cfg.nl_ltd = nl_ltd
    23	    if noise_mode is not None:
    24	        cfg.noise_mode = noise_mode
    25
    26	    # CRITICAL FIX: Push NL overrides into the actual model config
    27	    for module in bundle.model.modules():
    28	        if hasattr(module, 'config'):
    29	            if nl_ltp is not None:
    30	                module.config.NL_LTP = nl_ltp
    31	            if nl_ltd is not None:
    32	                module.config.NL_LTD = nl_ltd
    33	            if noise_mode is not None:
    34	                module.config.noise_mode = noise_mode
    35
    36	    instance_accs = []
    37	    print(f"\nEvaluating {exp_id} from {checkpoint_path} on {num_instances} fresh instances...")
    38	    print(f"  Config: NL_LTP={cfg.nl_ltp}, NL_LTD={cfg.nl_ltd}, noise_mode={cfg.noise_mode}")
    39
    40	    for instance_idx in range(num_instances):
    41	        seed = 42 + instance_idx * 100
    42	        torch.manual_seed(seed)
    43	        np.random.seed(seed)
    44
    45	        set_uniform_noise(bundle.model, sigma_c2c=cfg.sigma_c2c, sigma_d2d=cfg.sigma_d2d,
    46	                          noise_enabled=cfg.noise_enabled, resample_d2d=True, noise_mode=cfg.noise_mode)
    47
    48	        stats = run_mc_eval(bundle, eval_runs=mc_runs_per_instance, label=f"Instance {instance_idx+1}/{num_instances}")
    49	        instance_accs.append(stats["test_acc_mean"])
    50	        print(f"  Instance {instance_idx+1}: {stats['test_acc_mean']:.2f}%")
    51
    52	    mean_acc = float(np.mean(instance_accs))
    53	    std_acc = float(np.std(instance_accs))
    54	    print(f"Result: Mean={mean_acc:.4f}%, Std={std_acc:.4f}%, Range={min(instance_accs):.2f}--{max(instance_accs):.2f}%\n")
    55
    56	    return {
    57	        "checkpoint_path": str(checkpoint_path),
    58	        "exp_id": exp_id,
    59	        "fresh_instances": num_instances,
    60	        "mc_runs_per_instance": mc_runs_per_instance,
    61	        "nl_ltp": cfg.nl_ltp,
    62	        "nl_ltd": cfg.nl_ltd,
    63	        "noise_mode": cfg.noise_mode,
    64	        "cross_instance_mean": mean_acc,
    65	        "cross_instance_std": std_acc,
    66	        "instance_means": instance_accs,
    67	    }
    68
    69	def main():
    70	    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    71	    parser.add_argument("--checkpoint", required=True)
    72	    parser.add_argument("--exp-id", default="V4")
    73	    parser.add_argument("--model-type", default="tinyvit")
    74	    parser.add_argument("--device", default=None)
    75	    parser.add_argument("--num-instances", type=int, default=10)
    76	    parser.add_argument("--mc-runs", type=int, default=5)
    77	    parser.add_argument("--nl-ltp", type=float, default=None)
    78	    parser.add_argument("--nl-ltd", type=float, default=None)
    79	    parser.add_argument("--noise-mode", default=None)
    80	    parser.add_argument("--output", default=None)
    81	    args = parser.parse_args()
    82
    83	    device = args.device or ("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
    84	    result = evaluate_on_fresh_instances(
    85	        args.model_type, args.exp_id, args.checkpoint, device,
    86	        args.num_instances, args.mc_runs,
    87	        args.nl_ltp, args.nl_ltd, args.noise_mode
    88	    )
    89
    90	    output_path = args.output or f"report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/{Path(args.checkpoint).stem}_fresh_eval.json"
    91	    Path(output_path).parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    92	    with open(output_path, "w") as f:
    93	        json.dump(result, f, indent=2)
    94	    print(f"Saved to {output_path}")
    95
    96	if __name__ == "__main__":
    97	    main()

./report_md/_gpt/KIMI_CANONICAL_SURVIVORS_20260423.md:12:| **V4 same-instance (train best)** | 91.13% | Checkpoint metadata: `nl_ltp=1.0`, `nl_ltd=-1.0`, `use_second_order_ste=False`. NL=1 makes both bugs no-ops. |
./report_md/_gpt/KIMI_CANONICAL_SURVIVORS_20260423.md:13:| **V4 same-instance MC eval** | ~91.3% | Same checkpoint as above. Eval path is forward-only; backward bugs irrelevant. |
./report_md/_gpt/KIMI_CANONICAL_SURVIVORS_20260423.md:25:| K4R same-instance (91.62%) | **INVALID** — trained with branch swap + extraneous `nl` multiplier |
report_md/_gpt/AGENT_INTERCOM_HUB_20260423.md:91:- `scripts/_gpt/analyze_weight_distribution.py` compares standard-HAT vs Ensemble-HAT checkpoints, computes per-layer histograms and spectral norms, saves plots plus JSON, and hard-gates on the presence of the R1 best checkpoint unless `--force` is passed. Default checkpoint inputs are `checkpoints/V4_hybrid_standard_noise_hat_best.pt` and `checkpoints/_ensemble/V4_hybrid_standard_noise_hat_best.pt`.
./report_md/_gpt/KIMI_WORK1_LOOP_CLOSURE_ANALYSIS_20260423.md:40:| J1c | Groupwise NL mitigation | 91% same-instance, 38% fresh | Mitigation insufficient |
./report_md/_gpt/KIMI_WORK1_LOOP_CLOSURE_ANALYSIS_20260423.md:78:| Same-instance best | 91.50% | R1 training log | High |
./report_md/_gpt/KIMI_DEFENSE_SLIDES_CONTENT_20260423.md:122:- **Fresh-instance transfer evaluation:** After training, the checkpoint is evaluated on 10 unseen hardware instances × 5 Monte Carlo passes each, separating same-instance robustness from true deployment generalization.
./report_md/_gpt/KIMI_DEFENSE_SLIDES_CONTENT_20260423.md:123:- **The critical distinction:** Same-instance robustness and fresh-instance robustness are fundamentally different evaluation targets—standard HAT excels at the former but fails catastrophically at the latter.
./report_md/_gpt/KIMI_DEFENSE_SLIDES_CONTENT_20260423.md:126:- Standard HAT source-domain accuracy (same-instance, V4): **~91.6%** **[INVALID pre-Branch-A]** — awaiting K4R re-anchor
./report_md/_gpt/KIMI_DEFENSE_SLIDES_CONTENT_20260423.md:246:- **Methodology contribution:** A structured HAT taxonomy (cadence × noise profile) that enables rigorous attribution of accuracy loss to physical causes—distinguishing same-instance compensation from true deployment invariance.
report_md/_gpt/KIMI_STOPLOSS_BROADCAST_TEMPLATE_20260423.md:17:Because both bugs alter gradient scaling during training, the same-instance train accuracy (91.62%) is also contaminated. **No number from the K4R experiment can be cited.**

./report_md/审稿人意见-4.10.md:1032:* The manuscript distinguishes same-instance robustness from fresh-instance transfer, and the Ensemble HAT result is one of the strongest and most useful observations in the paper.
./report_md/审稿人意见-4.10.md:1200:This is an intelligent, potentially useful manuscript with a clear niche: it attempts to bridge organic optoelectronic device characterization and modern vision-model deployment through a profile-driven CIM simulator. The manuscript’s strengths are its transparent scoping, its focus on fresh-instance transfer rather than only same-instance robustness, and its effort to treat organic-specific frontend and retention effects inside a modern ML workflow. However, several issues prevent acceptance in the present form: the use of best test accuracy across epochs, incomplete seed statistics for headline claims, reliance on proxy-calibrated parameters for the central case study, insufficient validation of the “profile-driven” premise, and an energy story that is informative but too idealized for strong conclusions. I would encourage a substantially revised version, but for **Nature Communications** specifically, the paper is **not yet sufficiently validated or statistically mature**. The clearest path forward is to tighten experimental protocol, expand seed-based statistics, sharpen the boundary between simulation and evidence, and provide much stronger matched validation against measured profiles and/or established simulators.

./_archive/paper-drafts/05_results.md:37:Experiments on fresh hardware instances reveal a severe weakness in current transformer deployment. Tiny-ViT V4 collapses to chance-level performance (`10.00%`) when evaluated on arrays with a different fixed D2D realization, showing that same-instance robustness and fresh-instance robustness are fundamentally different evaluation targets and motivating multi-instance HAT as the next training step.

./_archive/paper-drafts/01_introduction.md:11:The paper is organized around four deployment-facing questions. First, is nominal conductance quantization really the dominant bottleneck once scale recovery and hardware-aware training are enabled? Second, what fails first as tasks become harder: quantization, converters, instance shift, or richer device physics? Third, is same-instance robustness enough, or does deployment collapse on fresh hardware realizations? Fourth, which physical stress regimes can be retrained around, and which ones remain hard boundaries for the current recipe? We treat the framework as a first-order behavioral simulator rather than a routed-chip emulator. IR drop, sneak paths, temperature fluctuations, differential-pair layout asymmetry, optical crosstalk, and pulse-faithful programming physics are therefore flagged as out-of-scope physical effects whose implications are revisited explicitly in Section 6. Our results show that quantization alone is often not the main limit in the canonical regime, while converter precision, complexity scaling, fresh-instance transfer, and richer physical stress dominate the practical risk. The strongest positive result is that Ensemble HAT raises fresh-instance Tiny-ViT accuracy from 10.00% to 86.37 ± 1.54%, while a literature-derived Zhang et al. (2025) OPECT profile transfers to 88.53% through the same interface. The strongest negative result is that severe nonlinear write remains a hard failure mode at 27.72 ± 0.82%, even after retraining.

./_archive/paper-drafts/07_conclusion.md:5:Three conclusions emerge most clearly. First, the impact of analog non-idealities is strongly regime-dependent. Under the canonical uniform-noise model, quantization and nominal stochastic noise are not the dominant accuracy bottlenecks, especially on CIFAR-10. Second, that apparent robustness does not generalize automatically to deployment: task complexity, fresh hardware instances, proportional state-dependent noise, and nonlinear write dynamics all expose much sharper failure modes. The most important corrective result in the paper is therefore not same-instance HAT, but Ensemble HAT, which raises fresh-instance Tiny-ViT accuracy from 10.00% to 86.37 ± 1.54%. Third, hardware-aware retraining can recover some matched physical regimes, such as proportional noise at 97.37 ± 0.05%, but it does not solve everything. Severe nonlinear-write distortion remains stuck at 27.72 ± 0.82% for the present first-order approximation, and the Flowers-102 boundary remains a hypothesis-generating failure case rather than a proved causal story.
./_archive/paper-drafts/06_discussion.md:54:The second direction is to improve robustness to hardware-instance shift. The zero-shot transfer experiments show that Tiny-ViT can overfit to a single D2D realization even when it is stable under same-instance noise. A natural remedy is **multi-instance HAT**, in which the training loop periodically resamples D2D masks rather than keeping a single fixed instance for the entire run. Such a strategy would directly target the failure mode exposed in Section 5.4 and is likely more valuable than simply increasing nominal noise magnitude during training.

./report_md/_gpt/CODEX_REVIEW_T6_T7_DRAFTS_20260423.md:55:> These ViT quantization papers provide context that low-bit transformer quantization is itself a hard problem. Our same-instance TinyViT/CIFAR-10 result shows that, in our controlled setting, the additional analog C2C/D2D perturbations do not dominate the source-domain accuracy loss. It does not by itself establish cross-instance robustness, which R1 shows remains weak.
./report_md/_gpt/CODEX_REVIEW_T6_T7_DRAFTS_20260423.md:59:> Digital quantization provides an important baseline for interpreting the degradation observed in our analog CIM setting. Prior ViT quantization work shows that transformer architectures are sensitive to low-bit deployment: FQ-ViT reports DeiT-B top-1 accuracy of `81.20%` when all modules are quantized to 8-bit and `80.85%` when attention maps are compressed to 4-bit, compared with `81.85%` full precision; PTQ4ViT reports near-lossless 8-bit PTQ but still around `2.1%` average degradation at 6-bit and much poorer fully 4-bit PTQ without mixed precision or correction; Q-ViT further shows that pushing ViTs to 3- or 4-bit typically requires quantization-aware training with component-wise bit allocation. These results suggest that low-bit transformer deployment is already a nontrivial baseline even before analog device noise is introduced. In our TinyViT/CIFAR-10 source-domain setting, the small drop from FP32 to 4-bit noisy inference indicates that C2C/D2D perturbations are not the dominant same-instance bottleneck. Cross-instance transfer remains a separate failure mode and must be evaluated with fresh D2D realizations rather than inferred from source accuracy.

./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/03_methods.md:143:The primary generalization metric is fresh-instance accuracy: after training concludes, the checkpoint is evaluated on $n=10$ independent unseen D2D realizations ("arrays"), with $5$ Monte Carlo forward passes per instance. The primary metric is the mean accuracy across the $10$ per-instance means; uncertainty is reported as the standard deviation across the $10$ per-instance means. Secondary metrics include source-domain (in-distribution) accuracy and per-instance variance. This protocol distinguishes same-instance overfitting from genuine distributional generalization.

./_archive/scripts-oneshot/append_kimi_km14.py:9:    f.write('\n## [Kimi] 2026-04-11 16:05 — KM3: Conclusion vs Results 一致性\n### Status\n- 完成\n### Findings\n| Conclusion Claim | Results 支撑 | 一致 |\n|:--|:--|:--:|\n| Ensemble HAT: 10.00% -> 86.37±1.54% | Section 5.8 Task 37 | ✅ |\n| proportional noise: 97.37±0.05% | Section 5.8 Task 34&36 | ✅ |\n| nonlinear write: 27.72±0.82% | Section 5.8 Task 35 | ✅ |\n| Zhang 2026 OPECT: 88.53% | Section 5.11 Case Study | ✅ |\n| energy: first-order, upper-bound-like | Section 5.10 + Discussion 6.4 | ✅ |\n- ✅ 无 overclaim: 所有 Conclusion 断言有 Results 数据支撑\n- ✅ 无重要遗漏: Ensemble HAT, proportional noise, nonlinear-write boundary, Zhang 2026 transfer, energy qualifier 均已涵盖\n- ✅ 11.45x claim 在 Conclusion 中有 qualifier\n- ✅ Conclusion 已更新: "same-instance HAT alone" (非 "same-instance HAT"), "distribution-matched" 加入了 proportional noise 描述\n### Recommended Fixes\n- 无需修改\n### Evidence\n- `paper/latex_gpt/sections/07_conclusion.tex` vs `paper/latex_gpt/sections/05_results.tex`\n')
./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/01_intro.md:5:The gap between device-level promise and system-level performance is dominated by non-ideality. Analog memory cells suffer from cell-to-cell (C2C) variability, device-to-device (D2D) mismatch, retention drift, and—most critically for this study—nonlinear write dynamics. In a nonlinear write regime, the programmed conductance deviates from the target weight in a state-dependent fashion, distorting the effective linear transform that the crossbar is supposed to implement. Unlike additive thermal noise, write nonlinearity introduces multiplicative, input-dependent perturbations that standard training pipelines do not naturally absorb. Hardware-aware training (HAT) mitigates some of these effects by injecting device noise into the forward training path, yet the standard practice of fixing a single D2D mismatch map throughout training invites a subtler failure: the model memorizes one hardware instance rather than learning to generalize across the deployment distribution. When the trained checkpoint encounters a fresh fabricated array with a different fixed mismatch realization, accuracy can collapse to chance level. This hardware-instance overfitting is especially pernicious because it is invisible during conventional same-instance validation, where the model is tested on the same noisy realization it trained against.

./paper/paper2/draft_v0/01_introduction.md:5:The gap between device-level promise and system-level performance is dominated by non-ideality. Analog memory cells suffer from cell-to-cell (C2C) variability, device-to-device (D2D) mismatch, retention drift, and—most critically for this study—nonlinear write dynamics. In a nonlinear write regime, the programmed conductance deviates from the target weight in a state-dependent fashion, distorting the effective linear transform that the crossbar is supposed to implement. Unlike additive thermal noise, write nonlinearity introduces multiplicative, input-dependent perturbations that standard training pipelines do not naturally absorb. Hardware-aware training (HAT) mitigates some of these effects by injecting device noise into the forward training path, yet the standard practice of fixing a single D2D mismatch map throughout training invites a subtler failure: the model memorizes one hardware instance rather than learning to generalize across the deployment distribution. When the trained checkpoint encounters a fresh fabricated array with a different fixed mismatch realization, accuracy can collapse to chance level. This hardware-instance overfitting is especially pernicious because it is invisible during conventional same-instance validation, where the model is tested on the same noisy realization it trained against.

./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:37:The protocol is implemented as follows. For instance $i \in \{0, \dots, 9\}$, the random seed is set to $42 + 100i$; this deterministic offset ensures reproducibility while guaranteeing independence across instances. The model architecture and checkpoint weights are reloaded, `resample_d2d_noise()` is called on every `AnalogLinear` and `AnalogConv2d` module to redraw the fixed mismatch buffers, and `set_noise_for_eval()` pushes the evaluation noise parameters into each layer. The test set is CIFAR-10 (10 000 images, 10 classes). No test-time augmentation, model averaging, or checkpoint ensembling is applied. Because the training D2D pattern is never reused at evaluation, the protocol measures true zero-shot hardware transfer rather than optimistic same-instance performance.
report_md/_gpt/CODEX_CROSS_REVIEW_RERUN_20260423.md:25:1. `eval_fresh_instances_postfix.py` does not automatically read NL values from checkpoint metadata. It evaluates with correct NL only if called with `--nl-ltp 2.0 --nl-ltd -2.0`.
report_md/_gpt/CODEX_CROSS_REVIEW_RERUN_20260423.md:26:2. `eval_fresh_instances_postfix.py` builds/evaluates via `train_tinyvit.py`, not `train_tinyvit_ensemble.py`. This currently loads the V4 checkpoint and uses corrected `analog_layers.py`, but it is a needless cross-path dependency.

report_md/_gpt/CODEX_CROSS_REVIEW_RERUN_20260423.md:212:## 3. Evaluation Script Audit: `eval_fresh_instances_postfix.py`
report_md/_gpt/CODEX_CROSS_REVIEW_RERUN_20260423.md:220:- Loaded `checkpoints/_gpt/postfix_reruns/V4_hybrid_standard_noise_hat_best.pt`.
report_md/_gpt/CODEX_CROSS_REVIEW_RERUN_20260423.md:237:- If `eval_fresh_instances_postfix.py` is run without `--nl-ltp 2.0 --nl-ltd -2.0`, it will evaluate the post-fix NL=2 checkpoint with default evaluator NL `(1.0, -1.0)`. That would be invalid.
report_md/_gpt/CODEX_CROSS_REVIEW_RERUN_20260423.md:253:`eval_fresh_instances_postfix.py` imports `load_model_bundle()` from `inference_analysis_utils.py`. For `model_type="tinyvit"`, that utility uses `train_tinyvit.py` for `build_model`, `evaluate`, and dataloaders (`inference_analysis_utils.py:45-50`, `326-373`), not `train_tinyvit_ensemble.py`.
report_md/_gpt/CODEX_CROSS_REVIEW_RERUN_20260423.md:267:`eval_fresh_instances_postfix.py` has no `--amp` flag and does not pass `amp_enabled=True` into `load_model_bundle()`. Therefore fresh-instance evaluation defaults to full precision.

report_md/审稿人意见-4.10.md:1032:* The manuscript distinguishes same-instance robustness from fresh-instance transfer, and the Ensemble HAT result is one of the strongest and most useful observations in the paper.
report_md/审稿人意见-4.10.md:1200:This is an intelligent, potentially useful manuscript with a clear niche: it attempts to bridge organic optoelectronic device characterization and modern vision-model deployment through a profile-driven CIM simulator. The manuscript’s strengths are its transparent scoping, its focus on fresh-instance transfer rather than only same-instance robustness, and its effort to treat organic-specific frontend and retention effects inside a modern ML workflow. However, several issues prevent acceptance in the present form: the use of best test accuracy across epochs, incomplete seed statistics for headline claims, reliance on proxy-calibrated parameters for the central case study, insufficient validation of the “profile-driven” premise, and an energy story that is informative but too idealized for strong conclusions. I would encourage a substantially revised version, but for **Nature Communications** specifically, the paper is **not yet sufficiently validated or statistically mature**. The clearest path forward is to tighten experimental protocol, expand seed-based statistics, sharpen the boundary between simulation and evidence, and provide much stronger matched validation against measured profiles and/or established simulators.

report_md/审稿人意见from_model.md:731:Generalization of Ensemble HAT: You show that Ensemble HAT solves the "fresh instance" problem. However, does this come at the cost of reduced model capacity or accuracy ceiling in the "same-instance" scenario? Did you observe any trade-off in the training dynamics?
report_md/审稿人意见from_model.md:739:This is a timely and potentially impactful paper that fills a methodological gap in the neuromorphic computing community. The framework is well-constructed, and the analysis of "what breaks first" in organic CIM is insightful, particularly the distinction between same-instance and fresh-instance robustness.

report_md/_gpt/CODEX_REVIEW_T6_T7_DRAFTS_20260423.md:55:> These ViT quantization papers provide context that low-bit transformer quantization is itself a hard problem. Our same-instance TinyViT/CIFAR-10 result shows that, in our controlled setting, the additional analog C2C/D2D perturbations do not dominate the source-domain accuracy loss. It does not by itself establish cross-instance robustness, which R1 shows remains weak.
report_md/_gpt/CODEX_REVIEW_T6_T7_DRAFTS_20260423.md:59:> Digital quantization provides an important baseline for interpreting the degradation observed in our analog CIM setting. Prior ViT quantization work shows that transformer architectures are sensitive to low-bit deployment: FQ-ViT reports DeiT-B top-1 accuracy of `81.20%` when all modules are quantized to 8-bit and `80.85%` when attention maps are compressed to 4-bit, compared with `81.85%` full precision; PTQ4ViT reports near-lossless 8-bit PTQ but still around `2.1%` average degradation at 6-bit and much poorer fully 4-bit PTQ without mixed precision or correction; Q-ViT further shows that pushing ViTs to 3- or 4-bit typically requires quantization-aware training with component-wise bit allocation. These results suggest that low-bit transformer deployment is already a nontrivial baseline even before analog device noise is introduced. In our TinyViT/CIFAR-10 source-domain setting, the small drop from FP32 to 4-bit noisy inference indicates that C2C/D2D perturbations are not the dominant same-instance bottleneck. Cross-instance transfer remains a separate failure mode and must be evaluated with fresh D2D realizations rather than inferred from source accuracy.

report_md/_gpt/BROADCAST_ENSEMBLE_HAT_FRESH_EVAL_20260423.md:52:**Conclusion:** Ensemble HAT solves the hardware-instance overfitting problem. Even under severe write nonlinearity (NL=2.0), fresh-instance transfer matches same-instance accuracy to within statistical noise.

report_md/_gpt/archive/old_reviewer_prep/JSON_CONSISTENCY_20260419.md:210:All of these files use `checkpoints/V4_hybrid_standard_noise_hat_best.pt` (`checkpoint_best_acc = 91.94`) but evaluation yields chance-level accuracy. This strongly suggests a **profile-to-checkpoint mismatch** or a bug in the evaluation script used for these runs (e.g., wrong `num_classes`, missing normalization, or ADC frontend not applied correctly).

codex
The evaluator mutates both the loaded experiment config and each module’s `AnalogLinearConfig` before fresh resampling. I’m checking the helper it calls next, because that determines whether the NL override is preserved or accidentally reset during `set_uniform_noise`.

     1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2	"""Inference harness for post-training Monte-Carlo evaluation and device-profile sweeps.
     3
     4	This module provides the glue between trained checkpoints and analog non-ideality
     5	models: loading a ``ModelBundle``, applying a ``DeviceProfile``, running repeated
     6	evaluations (``run_mc_eval``), and calibrating ADC ranges.  It is the primary
     7	entry point for reproducing the Fig. 4 accuracy-versus-condition bars.
     8	"""
     9
    10	from __future__ import annotations
    11
    12	import csv
    13	import inspect
    14	import json
    15	import math
    16	import os
    17	from dataclasses import dataclass
    18	from statistics import mean, stdev
    19	from typing import Dict, Iterable, List, Optional, Sequence, Tuple

    22	import torch.nn as nn
    23
    24	from analog_layers import (
    25	    ADCConfig,
    26	    ADCQuantizer,
    27	    AnalogConv2d,
    28	    AnalogLinear,
    29	    disable_sparsity_tracking,
    30	    enable_sparsity_tracking,
    31	    get_sparsity_report,
    32	    reset_sparsity_tracking,
    33	    resample_d2d_buffers,
    34	)
    35	from device_profile_utils import DeviceProfile
    36	from amp_utils import amp_enabled_for_device, autocast_context
    37	from train_convnext import (
    38	    DATASET_STATS as CONVNEXT_DATASET_STATS,
    39	    build_model as build_convnext_model,
    40	    evaluate as evaluate_convnext,

    44	try:
    45	    from train_tinyvit import (

    47	        evaluate as evaluate_tinyvit,
    48	        get_dataloaders as get_tinyvit_dataloaders,
    49	        get_v_experiment_configs,
    50	    )
    51	except ImportError:

    53	    evaluate_tinyvit = None
    54	    get_tinyvit_dataloaders = None
    55	    get_v_experiment_configs = None

    57	try:
    58	    from train_tinyvit_ensemble import (

    65	    except ImportError:

    69
    70	ANALOG_MODULE_TYPES = (AnalogLinear, AnalogConv2d)
    71
    72
    73	@dataclass
    74	class ModelBundle:
    75	    """Container for a loaded checkpoint, config, dataloader and frontend."""
    76	    model_type: str
    77	    experiment: str
    78	    experiment_name: str
    79	    dataset: str
    80	    device: str
    81	    model: nn.Module
    82	    exp_cfg: object
    83	    testloader: object
    84	    criterion: nn.Module
    85	    frontend: Optional[nn.Module]
    86	    checkpoint_path: str
    87	    checkpoint_epoch: Optional[int]
    88	    checkpoint_best_acc: Optional[float]
    89	    amp_enabled: bool
    90
    91
    92	def ensure_parent_dir(path: Optional[str]):
    93	    """Create the parent directory of *path* if it does not exist."""
    94	    if not path:
    95	        return
    96	    parent = os.path.dirname(path)
    97	    if parent:
    98	        os.makedirs(parent, exist_ok=True)
    99
   100
   101	def iter_analog_modules(model: nn.Module):
   102	    """Yield (name, module) tuples for every analog layer in *model*."""
   103	    for name, module in model.named_modules():
   104	        if isinstance(module, ANALOG_MODULE_TYPES):
   105	            yield name, module
   106
   107
   108	def snapshot_analog_state(model: nn.Module) -> Dict[str, dict]:
   109	    """Capture a reversible snapshot of all analog-layer configurations."""
   110	    state = {}
   111	    for name, module in iter_analog_modules(model):
   112	        inl = getattr(module.config, "inl_table", None)
   113	        state[name] = {
   114	            "n_states": int(module.config.n_states),
   115	            "G_min": float(module.config.G_min),
   116	            "G_max": float(module.config.G_max),
   117	            "noise_enabled": bool(module.config.noise_enabled),
   118	            "noise_mode": str(getattr(module.config, "noise_mode", "uniform")),
   119	            "sigma_c2c": float(module.config.sigma_c2c),
   120	            "sigma_d2d": float(module.config.sigma_d2d),
   121	            "NL_LTP": float(getattr(module.config, "NL_LTP", 1.0)),
   122	            "NL_LTD": float(getattr(module.config, "NL_LTD", -1.0)),
   123	            "retention_enabled": bool(module.config.retention_enabled),
   124	            "inference_time": float(module.config.inference_time),
   125	            "retention_recalibrate_scale": bool(getattr(module.config, "retention_recalibrate_scale", False)),
   126	            "retention_scales_d2d": bool(getattr(module.config, "retention_scales_d2d", False)),
   127	            "inl_table": inl.detach().cpu().tolist() if isinstance(inl, torch.Tensor) else inl,
   128	        }
   129	    return state
   130
   131
   132	def restore_analog_state(model: nn.Module, state: Dict[str, dict]):
   133	    """Restore analog-layer configurations from a snapshot dict."""
   134	    for name, module in iter_analog_modules(model):
   135	        if name not in state:
   136	            continue
   137	        saved = state[name]
   138	        module.config.n_states = saved["n_states"]
   139	        module.config.G_min = saved["G_min"]
   140	        module.config.G_max = saved["G_max"]
   141	        module.config.noise_enabled = saved["noise_enabled"]
   142	        module.config.noise_mode = saved.get("noise_mode", "uniform")
   143	        module.config.sigma_c2c = saved["sigma_c2c"]
   144	        module.config.sigma_d2d = saved["sigma_d2d"]
   145	        module.config.NL_LTP = saved.get("NL_LTP", 1.0)
   146	        module.config.NL_LTD = saved.get("NL_LTD", -1.0)
   147	        module.config.retention_enabled = saved["retention_enabled"]
   148	        module.config.inference_time = saved["inference_time"]
   149	        module.config.retention_recalibrate_scale = saved.get("retention_recalibrate_scale", False)
   150	        module.config.retention_scales_d2d = saved.get("retention_scales_d2d", False)
   151	        saved_inl = saved.get("inl_table")
   152	        if saved_inl is None:
   153	            module.config.inl_table = None
   154	        else:
   155	            module.config.inl_table = torch.tensor(
   156	                saved_inl,
   157	                dtype=torch.float32,
   158	                device=module.weight.device,
   159	            )
   160
   161
   162	def set_uniform_noise(model: nn.Module, sigma_c2c: float, sigma_d2d: float,
   163	                      noise_enabled: bool, resample_d2d: bool = False,
   164	                      noise_mode: Optional[str] = None):
   165	    """Apply uniform C2C/D2D noise settings to every analog layer in *model*."""
   166	    for _, module in iter_analog_modules(model):
   167	        module.config.noise_enabled = noise_enabled
   168	        module.config.sigma_c2c = sigma_c2c
   169	        module.config.sigma_d2d = sigma_d2d
   170	        if noise_mode is not None:
   171	            module.config.noise_mode = noise_mode
   172	    if resample_d2d:
   173	        resample_d2d_buffers(model)
   174
   175
   176	def set_uniform_retention(model: nn.Module, inference_time: float,
   177	                          recalibrate_scale: bool = False,
   178	                          scale_d2d: bool = False):
   179	    """Configure retention decay parameters for all analog layers."""
   180	    for _, module in iter_analog_modules(model):
   181	        module.config.retention_enabled = inference_time > 0
   182	        module.config.inference_time = inference_time
   183	        module.config.retention_recalibrate_scale = recalibrate_scale
   184	        module.config.retention_scales_d2d = scale_d2d
   185
   186
   187	def apply_device_profile(model: nn.Module, profile: DeviceProfile,
   188	                         resample_d2d: bool = True) -> int:
   189	    """Push a literature or measured device profile into every analog layer."""
   190	    active = 0
   191	    for _, module in iter_analog_modules(model):
   192	        module.config.G_min = float(profile.G_min)
   193	        module.config.G_max = float(profile.G_max)
   194	        module.config.n_states = int(profile.n_states)
   195	        module.config.sigma_c2c = float(profile.sigma_c2c)
   196	        module.config.sigma_d2d = float(profile.sigma_d2d)
   197	        module.config.noise_mode = str(profile.noise_mode)
   198	        if profile.NL_LTP is not None:
   199	            module.config.NL_LTP = float(profile.NL_LTP)
   200	        if profile.NL_LTD is not None:
   201	            module.config.NL_LTD = float(profile.NL_LTD)
   202	        module.config.noise_enabled = (profile.sigma_c2c > 0.0) or (profile.sigma_d2d > 0.0)
   203	        if profile.tau_1 is not None:
   204	            module.config.tau_1 = float(profile.tau_1)
   205	        if profile.tau_2 is not None:
   206	            module.config.tau_2 = float(profile.tau_2)
   207	        if profile.A_0 is not None:
   208	            module.config.A_0 = float(profile.A_0)
   209	        if profile.inl_table is not None:
   210	            module.config.inl_table = torch.tensor(
   211	                profile.inl_table,
   212	                dtype=torch.float32,
   213	                device=module.weight.device,
   214	            )
   215	        else:
   216	            module.config.inl_table = None
   217	        active += 1
   218	    if resample_d2d:
   219	        resample_d2d_buffers(model)
   220	    return active
   221
   222
   223	def collect_analog_noise_diagnostics(model: nn.Module,
   224	                                     layer_names: Optional[Sequence[str]] = None) -> List[dict]:
   225	    """Return per-layer noise statistics for analog modules."""
   226	    requested = set(layer_names) if layer_names else None
   227	    rows: List[dict] = []
   228
   229	    for name, module in iter_analog_modules(model):
   230	        if requested is not None and name not in requested:
   231	            continue
   232	        with torch.no_grad():
   233	            weight = module.weight.float()
   234	            G_pos, G_neg = module._weight_to_conductance(module.weight)
   235	            conductance_weight = (G_pos - G_neg).float()
   236	            G_range = float(module.config.G_max - module.config.G_min)
   237	            noise_mode = str(getattr(module.config, "noise_mode", "uniform")).lower()
   238	            if noise_mode == "proportional":
   239	                reference = conductance_weight.abs().clamp_min(1e-8)
   240	                d2d_noise = (module.d2d_noise.float() / max(G_range, 1e-8)) * reference
   241	                c2c_noise = (
   242	                    torch.randn_like(conductance_weight) * module.config.sigma_c2c * reference
   243	                    if module.config.sigma_c2c > 0 else torch.zeros_like(conductance_weight)
   244	                )
   245	            else:
   246	                d2d_noise = module.d2d_noise.float()
   247	                c2c_noise = (
   248	                    torch.randn_like(conductance_weight) * module.config.sigma_c2c * G_range
   249	                    if module.config.sigma_c2c > 0 else torch.zeros_like(conductance_weight)
   250	                )
   251	            total_noise = d2d_noise + c2c_noise
   252	            scale = float(module._conductance_to_weight_scale(module.weight).float().item())
   253
   254	            def safe_std(tensor: torch.Tensor) -> float:
   255	                return float(tensor.float().std(unbiased=False).item())
   256
   257	            conductance_std = safe_std(conductance_weight)
   258	            weight_std = safe_std(weight)
   259	            effective_weight = conductance_weight * scale
   260	            effective_noise = total_noise * scale

codex
`set_uniform_noise()` only changes `noise_enabled`, `sigma_c2c`, `sigma_d2d`, and `noise_mode`; it does not overwrite `NL_LTP` or `NL_LTD`, so the evaluator’s NL override should survive the fresh-instance resampling path. Next I’m loading the checkpoint metadata and recomputing the stats from the stored `instance_means`.

/bin/bash -lc "/home/qiaosir/miniconda3/envs/LLM/bin/python - <<'PY'
import json, math, statistics
from pathlib import Path
p = Path('report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/postfix_ensemble_hat_v4_nl20_fresh_eval.json')
data = json.loads(p.read_text())
vals = [float(x) for x in data['instance_means']]
mean = sum(vals)/len(vals)
pop_var = sum((x-mean)**2 for x in vals)/len(vals)
pop_std = math.sqrt(pop_var)
samp_std = statistics.stdev(vals)
print('n=', len(vals))
print('vals=', ', '.join(f'{x:.6f}' for x in vals))
print('mean=', f'{mean:.12f}')
print('std_population=', f'{pop_std:.12f}')
print('std_sample=', f'{samp_std:.12f}')
print('min=', f'{min(vals):.6f}', 'max=', f'{max(vals):.6f}', 'range=', f'{max(vals)-min(vals):.6f}')
print('json_mean=', data.get('cross_instance_mean'))
print('json_std=', data.get('cross_instance_std'))
print('json_nl=', data.get('nl_ltp'), data.get('nl_ltd'))

import json, torch
from pathlib import Path
p = Path('checkpoints/_gpt/postfix_reruns/V4_hybrid_standard_noise_hat_best.pt')
ckpt = torch.load(p, map_location='cpu', weights_only=False)
print('keys=', sorted(ckpt.keys()))
for k in ['epoch','best_epoch','best_acc','dataset','num_classes','amp_enabled','checkpoint_path','source_checkpoint']:
    print(f'{k}=', ckpt.get(k))
exp = ckpt.get('exp_cfg') or {}
print('exp_cfg_subset=', json.dumps({k: exp.get(k) for k in ['name','use_hybrid','n_states','nl_ltp','nl_ltd','sigma_c2c','sigma_d2d','noise_mode','noise_enabled','hat_training','adc_bits','epochs','batch_size','lr','weight_decay']}, sort_keys=True))
hist = ckpt.get('history') or []
print('history_len=', len(hist))
if hist:
    print('history_first=', json.dumps(hist[0], sort_keys=True))
    print('history_last=', json.dumps(hist[-1], sort_keys=True))
    best_rows = [r for r in hist if abs(float(r.get('test_acc', -999))-float(ckpt.get('best_acc', -999))) < 1e-9]
    print('history_best_rows=', json.dumps(best_rows[-3:], sort_keys=True))
msd = ckpt.get('model_state_dict') or {}
print('model_state_dict_keys=', len(msd))
print('head.fc.weight_shape=', tuple(msd.get('head.fc.weight').shape) if 'head.fc.weight' in msd else None)

report_md/审稿人意见-4.10.md:1032:* The manuscript distinguishes same-instance robustness from fresh-instance transfer, and the Ensemble HAT result is one of the strongest and most useful observations in the paper.
report_md/审稿人意见-4.10.md:1200:This is an intelligent, potentially useful manuscript with a clear niche: it attempts to bridge organic optoelectronic device characterization and modern vision-model deployment through a profile-driven CIM simulator. The manuscript’s strengths are its transparent scoping, its focus on fresh-instance transfer rather than only same-instance robustness, and its effort to treat organic-specific frontend and retention effects inside a modern ML workflow. However, several issues prevent acceptance in the present form: the use of best test accuracy across epochs, incomplete seed statistics for headline claims, reliance on proxy-calibrated parameters for the central case study, insufficient validation of the “profile-driven” premise, and an energy story that is informative but too idealized for strong conclusions. I would encourage a substantially revised version, but for **Nature Communications** specifically, the paper is **not yet sufficiently validated or statistically mature**. The clearest path forward is to tighten experimental protocol, expand seed-based statistics, sharpen the boundary between simulation and evidence, and provide much stronger matched validation against measured profiles and/or established simulators.

report_md/Gemini.md:40:**Clarification**: C1 was not intentionally truncated to 2 epochs. The original training run crashed during C5 (CUDA error), which also interrupted C1 mid-flight. Based on the actual log (`train_convnext_restart_*.log`), C1 reached **epoch 109/200** with accuracy **88.96%** before the crash — not 47.89% from 2 epochs. The 47.89% figure in the JSON corresponds to an early smoke-test artifact.

report_md/_gpt/CODEX_REVIEW_T6_T7_DRAFTS_20260423.md:55:> These ViT quantization papers provide context that low-bit transformer quantization is itself a hard problem. Our same-instance TinyViT/CIFAR-10 result shows that, in our controlled setting, the additional analog C2C/D2D perturbations do not dominate the source-domain accuracy loss. It does not by itself establish cross-instance robustness, which R1 shows remains weak.
report_md/_gpt/CODEX_REVIEW_T6_T7_DRAFTS_20260423.md:59:> Digital quantization provides an important baseline for interpreting the degradation observed in our analog CIM setting. Prior ViT quantization work shows that transformer architectures are sensitive to low-bit deployment: FQ-ViT reports DeiT-B top-1 accuracy of `81.20%` when all modules are quantized to 8-bit and `80.85%` when attention maps are compressed to 4-bit, compared with `81.85%` full precision; PTQ4ViT reports near-lossless 8-bit PTQ but still around `2.1%` average degradation at 6-bit and much poorer fully 4-bit PTQ without mixed precision or correction; Q-ViT further shows that pushing ViTs to 3- or 4-bit typically requires quantization-aware training with component-wise bit allocation. These results suggest that low-bit transformer deployment is already a nontrivial baseline even before analog device noise is introduced. In our TinyViT/CIFAR-10 source-domain setting, the small drop from FP32 to 4-bit noisy inference indicates that C2C/D2D perturbations are not the dominant same-instance bottleneck. Cross-instance transfer remains a separate failure mode and must be evaluated with fresh D2D realizations rather than inferred from source accuracy.
report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/iso_accuracy_contour_data.json:1067:    "mean": 82.26700000000001,
./_archive/paper-drafts/05_results.md:37:Experiments on fresh hardware instances reveal a severe weakness in current transformer deployment. Tiny-ViT V4 collapses to chance-level performance (`10.00%`) when evaluated on arrays with a different fixed D2D realization, showing that same-instance robustness and fresh-instance robustness are fundamentally different evaluation targets and motivating multi-instance HAT as the next training step.
./_archive/paper-drafts/01_introduction.md:11:The paper is organized around four deployment-facing questions. First, is nominal conductance quantization really the dominant bottleneck once scale recovery and hardware-aware training are enabled? Second, what fails first as tasks become harder: quantization, converters, instance shift, or richer device physics? Third, is same-instance robustness enough, or does deployment collapse on fresh hardware realizations? Fourth, which physical stress regimes can be retrained around, and which ones remain hard boundaries for the current recipe? We treat the framework as a first-order behavioral simulator rather than a routed-chip emulator. IR drop, sneak paths, temperature fluctuations, differential-pair layout asymmetry, optical crosstalk, and pulse-faithful programming physics are therefore flagged as out-of-scope physical effects whose implications are revisited explicitly in Section 6. Our results show that quantization alone is often not the main limit in the canonical regime, while converter precision, complexity scaling, fresh-instance transfer, and richer physical stress dominate the practical risk. The strongest positive result is that Ensemble HAT raises fresh-instance Tiny-ViT accuracy from 10.00% to 86.37 ± 1.54%, while a literature-derived Zhang et al. (2025) OPECT profile transfers to 88.53% through the same interface. The strongest negative result is that severe nonlinear write remains a hard failure mode at 27.72 ± 0.82%, even after retraining.
./run_postfix_eval.sh:8:CHECKPOINT=$PROJECT/checkpoints/_gpt/postfix_reruns/V4_hybrid_standard_noise_hat_best.pt
./run_postfix_eval.sh:20:# 1. Same-instance evaluation (sanity check)
./run_postfix_eval.sh:21:echo "1. Same-instance evaluation..."
./run_postfix_eval.sh:33:    --output report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/postfix_ensemble_hat_v4_nl20_fresh_eval.json
./_archive/paper-drafts/07_conclusion.md:5:Three conclusions emerge most clearly. First, the impact of analog non-idealities is strongly regime-dependent. Under the canonical uniform-noise model, quantization and nominal stochastic noise are not the dominant accuracy bottlenecks, especially on CIFAR-10. Second, that apparent robustness does not generalize automatically to deployment: task complexity, fresh hardware instances, proportional state-dependent noise, and nonlinear write dynamics all expose much sharper failure modes. The most important corrective result in the paper is therefore not same-instance HAT, but Ensemble HAT, which raises fresh-instance Tiny-ViT accuracy from 10.00% to 86.37 ± 1.54%. Third, hardware-aware retraining can recover some matched physical regimes, such as proportional noise at 97.37 ± 0.05%, but it does not solve everything. Severe nonlinear-write distortion remains stuck at 27.72 ± 0.82% for the present first-order approximation, and the Flowers-102 boundary remains a hypothesis-generating failure case rather than a proved causal story.
report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/postfix_ensemble_hat_v4_nl20_fresh_eval.json:2:  "checkpoint_path": "checkpoints/_gpt/postfix_reruns/V4_hybrid_standard_noise_hat_best.pt",
./_archive/paper-drafts/06_discussion.md:54:The second direction is to improve robustness to hardware-instance shift. The zero-shot transfer experiments show that Tiny-ViT can overfit to a single D2D realization even when it is stable under same-instance noise. A natural remedy is **multi-instance HAT**, in which the training loop periodically resamples D2D masks rather than keeping a single fixed instance for the entire run. Such a strategy would directly target the failure mode exposed in Section 5.4 and is likely more valuable than simply increasing nominal noise magnitude during training.

report_md/_gpt/AGENT_SYNC_gpt.md:3743:- §6.X.2 "Array-Level Uniformity Over Single-Device Variability" — Task 12: same-instance 91.6% vs fresh-instance 10%
report_md/_gpt/AGENT_SYNC_gpt.md:3964:**Projection**: At current rate of ~+2 pp per 5 epochs, V3 might reach ~55-65% by epoch 100. This would yield a V1→V3 degradation of **-22 to -32 pp** on CIFAR-100, compared to only **-7.9 pp** on CIFAR-10.
report_md/_gpt/AGENT_SYNC_gpt.md:4065:**V1 = 97.97%** — Tiny-ViT excels on Flowers-102 in FP32 (small dataset, pretrained backbone, fast convergence at epoch 10).

./report_md/Gemini.md:40:**Clarification**: C1 was not intentionally truncated to 2 epochs. The original training run crashed during C5 (CUDA error), which also interrupted C1 mid-flight. Based on the actual log (`train_convnext_restart_*.log`), C1 reached **epoch 109/200** with accuracy **88.96%** before the crash — not 47.89% from 2 epochs. The 47.89% figure in the JSON corresponds to an early smoke-test artifact.
./report_md/Gemini.md:362:**Clarification**: C1 was not intentionally truncated to 2 epochs. The original training run crashed during C5 (CUDA error), which also interrupted C1 mid-flight. Based on the actual log (`train_convnext_restart_*.log`), C1 reached **epoch 109/200** with accuracy **88.96%** before the crash — not 47.89% from 2 epochs. The 47.89% figure in the JSON corresponds to an early smoke-test artifact.

./report_md/审稿人意见-4.10.md:1032:* The manuscript distinguishes same-instance robustness from fresh-instance transfer, and the Ensemble HAT result is one of the strongest and most useful observations in the paper.
./report_md/审稿人意见-4.10.md:1200:This is an intelligent, potentially useful manuscript with a clear niche: it attempts to bridge organic optoelectronic device characterization and modern vision-model deployment through a profile-driven CIM simulator. The manuscript’s strengths are its transparent scoping, its focus on fresh-instance transfer rather than only same-instance robustness, and its effort to treat organic-specific frontend and retention effects inside a modern ML workflow. However, several issues prevent acceptance in the present form: the use of best test accuracy across epochs, incomplete seed statistics for headline claims, reliance on proxy-calibrated parameters for the central case study, insufficient validation of the “profile-driven” premise, and an energy story that is informative but too idealized for strong conclusions. I would encourage a substantially revised version, but for **Nature Communications** specifically, the paper is **not yet sufficiently validated or statistically mature**. The clearest path forward is to tighten experimental protocol, expand seed-based statistics, sharpen the boundary between simulation and evidence, and provide much stronger matched validation against measured profiles and/or established simulators.
./report_md/审稿人意见from_model.md:731:Generalization of Ensemble HAT: You show that Ensemble HAT solves the "fresh instance" problem. However, does this come at the cost of reduced model capacity or accuracy ceiling in the "same-instance" scenario? Did you observe any trade-off in the training dynamics?
./report_md/审稿人意见from_model.md:739:This is a timely and potentially impactful paper that fills a methodological gap in the neuromorphic computing community. The framework is well-constructed, and the analysis of "what breaks first" in organic CIM is insightful, particularly the distinction between same-instance and fresh-instance robustness.
report_md/_gpt/archive/round_p_rescinded/GEMINI_REDTEAM_V2_20260421.md:14:**Defense:** The manuscript reports ~30% fresh-instance plateaus across MLP-linear, all-linear, and joint recipes, with curves flatlining before epoch 100; the 32.12% MLP-only result never climbs even as source accuracy hits 87.79%. This is an optimization barrier, not a budget shortage.

./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/03_methods.md:143:The primary generalization metric is fresh-instance accuracy: after training concludes, the checkpoint is evaluated on $n=10$ independent unseen D2D realizations ("arrays"), with $5$ Monte Carlo forward passes per instance. The primary metric is the mean accuracy across the $10$ per-instance means; uncertainty is reported as the standard deviation across the $10$ per-instance means. Secondary metrics include source-domain (in-distribution) accuracy and per-instance variance. This protocol distinguishes same-instance overfitting from genuine distributional generalization.

./paper/paper2/skeleton_v0/01_intro.md:5:The gap between device-level promise and system-level performance is dominated by non-ideality. Analog memory cells suffer from cell-to-cell (C2C) variability, device-to-device (D2D) mismatch, retention drift, and—most critically for this study—nonlinear write dynamics. In a nonlinear write regime, the programmed conductance deviates from the target weight in a state-dependent fashion, distorting the effective linear transform that the crossbar is supposed to implement. Unlike additive thermal noise, write nonlinearity introduces multiplicative, input-dependent perturbations that standard training pipelines do not naturally absorb. Hardware-aware training (HAT) mitigates some of these effects by injecting device noise into the forward training path, yet the standard practice of fixing a single D2D mismatch map throughout training invites a subtler failure: the model memorizes one hardware instance rather than learning to generalize across the deployment distribution. When the trained checkpoint encounters a fresh fabricated array with a different fixed mismatch realization, accuracy can collapse to chance level. This hardware-instance overfitting is especially pernicious because it is invisible during conventional same-instance validation, where the model is tested on the same noisy realization it trained against.

./paper/paper2/draft_v0/01_introduction.md:5:The gap between device-level promise and system-level performance is dominated by non-ideality. Analog memory cells suffer from cell-to-cell (C2C) variability, device-to-device (D2D) mismatch, retention drift, and—most critically for this study—nonlinear write dynamics. In a nonlinear write regime, the programmed conductance deviates from the target weight in a state-dependent fashion, distorting the effective linear transform that the crossbar is supposed to implement. Unlike additive thermal noise, write nonlinearity introduces multiplicative, input-dependent perturbations that standard training pipelines do not naturally absorb. Hardware-aware training (HAT) mitigates some of these effects by injecting device noise into the forward training path, yet the standard practice of fixing a single D2D mismatch map throughout training invites a subtler failure: the model memorizes one hardware instance rather than learning to generalize across the deployment distribution. When the trained checkpoint encounters a fresh fabricated array with a different fixed mismatch realization, accuracy can collapse to chance level. This hardware-instance overfitting is especially pernicious because it is invisible during conventional same-instance validation, where the model is tested on the same noisy realization it trained against.

./paper/paper2/draft_v0/03_methods.md:37:The protocol is implemented as follows. For instance $i \in \{0, \dots, 9\}$, the random seed is set to $42 + 100i$; this deterministic offset ensures reproducibility while guaranteeing independence across instances. The model architecture and checkpoint weights are reloaded, `resample_d2d_noise()` is called on every `AnalogLinear` and `AnalogConv2d` module to redraw the fixed mismatch buffers, and `set_noise_for_eval()` pushes the evaluation noise parameters into each layer. The test set is CIFAR-10 (10 000 images, 10 classes). No test-time augmentation, model averaging, or checkpoint ensembling is applied. Because the training D2D pattern is never reused at evaluation, the protocol measures true zero-shot hardware transfer rather than optimistic same-instance performance.

./report_md/_gpt/CODEX_REVIEW_T6_T7_DRAFTS_20260423.md:55:> These ViT quantization papers provide context that low-bit transformer quantization is itself a hard problem. Our same-instance TinyViT/CIFAR-10 result shows that, in our controlled setting, the additional analog C2C/D2D perturbations do not dominate the source-domain accuracy loss. It does not by itself establish cross-instance robustness, which R1 shows remains weak.
./report_md/_gpt/CODEX_REVIEW_T6_T7_DRAFTS_20260423.md:59:> Digital quantization provides an important baseline for interpreting the degradation observed in our analog CIM setting. Prior ViT quantization work shows that transformer architectures are sensitive to low-bit deployment: FQ-ViT reports DeiT-B top-1 accuracy of `81.20%` when all modules are quantized to 8-bit and `80.85%` when attention maps are compressed to 4-bit, compared with `81.85%` full precision; PTQ4ViT reports near-lossless 8-bit PTQ but still around `2.1%` average degradation at 6-bit and much poorer fully 4-bit PTQ without mixed precision or correction; Q-ViT further shows that pushing ViTs to 3- or 4-bit typically requires quantization-aware training with component-wise bit allocation. These results suggest that low-bit transformer deployment is already a nontrivial baseline even before analog device noise is introduced. In our TinyViT/CIFAR-10 source-domain setting, the small drop from FP32 to 4-bit noisy inference indicates that C2C/D2D perturbations are not the dominant same-instance bottleneck. Cross-instance transfer remains a separate failure mode and must be evaluated with fresh D2D realizations rather than inferred from source accuracy.

/bin/bash -lc "/home/qiaosir/miniconda3/envs/LLM/bin/python - <<'PY'
import json, torch
from pathlib import Path
for rel in ['checkpoints/_gpt/postfix_reruns/V4_hybrid_standard_noise_hat_best.pt','checkpoints/_gpt/postfix_reruns/V4_hybrid_standard_noise_hat_last.pt']:
    p=Path(rel)
    ckpt=torch.load(p,map_location='cpu',weights_only=False)
    print('\\n==', rel)
    print('epoch', ckpt.get('epoch'), 'best_epoch', ckpt.get('best_epoch'), 'best_acc', ckpt.get('best_acc'), 'dataset', ckpt.get('dataset'), 'num_classes', ckpt.get('num_classes'), 'amp', ckpt.get('amp_enabled'))
    exp=ckpt.get('exp_cfg') or {}
    print('exp_cfg', json.dumps({k:exp.get(k) for k in ['name','use_hybrid','n_states','nl_ltp','nl_ltd','sigma_c2c','sigma_d2d','noise_mode','noise_enabled','hat_training','adc_bits','epochs','batch_size','lr','weight_decay']}, sort_keys=True))
    hist=ckpt.get('history')
    print('history_type', type(hist).__name__)
    if isinstance(hist, dict):
        print('history_keys', sorted(hist.keys()))
        lengths={k:len(v) for k,v in hist.items() if hasattr(v,'__len__') and not isinstance(v,(str,bytes))}
        print('history_lengths', lengths)
        for k,v in hist.items():
            if isinstance(v, list) and v:
                print(f'{k}: first={v[0]} last={v[-1]}')
    elif isinstance(hist, list):
        print('history_len', len(hist))
        if hist:
            print('history_first', hist[0])
            print('history_last', hist[-1])
    msd=ckpt.get('model_state_dict') or {}
    print('state_dict_keys', len(msd), 'head_shape', tuple(msd['head.fc.weight'].shape) if 'head.fc.weight' in msd else None)

report_md/_gpt/AGENT_INTERCOM_HUB_20260423.md:47:**Action:** Acknowledged current matrix from dispatch/sync: T0/R1 training is still active, T1 corrected energy JSON is available, T2 is documented as complete/failure-evidence, and T4/T5 remain gated on R1. Reviewed `report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/energy_scale_recovery_sensitivity.json`: arithmetic is internally consistent (`273.94 / 23.924890829694323 = 11.45`, `68.485 / 23.924890829694323 = 2.8625`), and the scale-recovery sweep only perturbs total energy negligibly through 1000 fJ. Main caveat is modeling, not arithmetic: the INT8 baseline is a first-order assumption (`digital_INT8_uJ = digital_FP32_uJ / 4`), so manuscript text should label `~2.86x vs INT8` as assumption-backed rather than measured hardware data. Checked `logs/_gpt/r1_clean_anchor_train_20260423_131610.log`: latest visible line is `2026-04-23 14:31:52`, Epoch `44/100`, `train_acc=98.10%`, `test_acc=88.86%`, `best=90.43%` (best reached by Epoch 34). No completion marker or failure trace in the log; status reads as ongoing with some eval fluctuation after the current best.
report_md/_gpt/AGENT_INTERCOM_HUB_20260423.md:83:**Action:** Re-verified `report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/energy_scale_recovery_sensitivity.json` with high-precision Python: `273.94 / 23.924890829694323 = 11.450000000000000...` and `68.485 / 23.924890829694323 = 2.862500000000000...`, so the reported `11.45x` and `2.8625x` round correctly. The scale-recovery sweep is also arithmetically correct: `E_sr = E_mult * 16704 ops`, with `E_sr_uJ = E_mult_fJ * 16704 * 1e-9`, matching every JSON row checked. While preparing T4/T5, I found a reproducibility hazard: working-tree `analog_layers.py` is currently dirty relative to clean commit `2bf59db30183edccd838c169e89c1539d1a4f907` (one local diff in the second-order correction branch), and `report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/r1_clean_anchor_fresh_eval.json` is still missing. I am adding hard preflight guards so the new scripts refuse to run unless the R1 gate is satisfied and `analog_layers.py` matches the clean baseline or the operator explicitly overrides it.
report_md/_gpt/AGENT_INTERCOM_HUB_20260423.md:84:**Blockers:** T4 remains gated because `r1_clean_anchor_fresh_eval.json` does not exist yet. Clean-engine guarantee is also blocked by local drift in `analog_layers.py` versus commit `2bf59db30183edccd838c169e89c1539d1a4f907`.
report_md/_gpt/AGENT_INTERCOM_HUB_20260423.md:89:- `scripts/_gpt/run_data_ablation_cifar10.py` now runs CIFAR-10 subset ablations for `--data-fraction {0.1,0.25,0.5,1.0}` across `{V3 no-HAT, V4 HAT}`, writes JSON summaries to `report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/`, and hard-gates execution on both `(a)` `report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/r1_clean_anchor_fresh_eval.json` with `cross_instance_mean > 70%` and `(b)` `analog_layers.py` exactly matching user-requested commit `2bf59db30183edccd838c169e89c1539d1a4f907` unless `--force` is passed.
report_md/_gpt/AGENT_INTERCOM_HUB_20260423.md:93:**Blockers:** T4 execution is still blocked because `report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/r1_clean_anchor_fresh_eval.json` is missing. Separate reproducibility note: repository `HEAD` advanced during this work from `2bf59db30183edccd838c169e89c1539d1a4f907` to `33bed9c` (`fix(analog_layers): second-order branch mapping must match first-order`), so the T4 launcher will now intentionally refuse to run on current `HEAD` unless the expected commit is updated or `--force` is used.
report_md/_gpt/AGENT_INTERCOM_HUB_20260423.md:115:**Action:** Read canonical `report_md/_gpt/json_gpt/r1_clean_anchor_fresh_eval.json`. Fresh-instance mean is `34.5612%` with std `8.7878%` (generated 2026-04-23). This keeps `R2` blocked by the existing `>70%` auto-chain gate. For post-R1 follow-up, I am **not** overriding the old `T4` data-ablation gate: the result is below the project’s own ambiguous band (`[35, 50)`), so `T4` is lower-priority GPU spend than the cheap mechanistic analysis. I am proceeding directly with `T5` weight-distribution analysis now.
